revision.text:
'''Ōkuninushi Mythology''' (大国主の神話) recounts the Japanese mythology surrounding [[Ōkuninushi]] following the tale of the [[Hare of Inaba]].
== Synopsis ==
=== Persecution by the Eighty Gods === {{ill|Ōnamuji|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}} (later known as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) was pursued by his elder brothers, the {{ill|Eighty Gods|ja|八十神|1=Yasogami|lt=Yasogami (80 gods)}}, who sought to marry {{ill|Yagami-hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}} of {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province }}. However, when Yagami-hime declared her intent to wed Ōnamuji instead, the Eighty Gods became enraged and plotted to kill him. They led Ōnamuji to the foothills of a mountain on the border of {{ill|Hōki Province|ja|伯岐国}}, telling him, "A red boar dwells in these mountains. We will drive it down, and you must capture it." As Ōnamuji waited, they instead rolled a burning boulder down the mountain, which he attempted to catch, only to be consumed by the flames and perish.<ref>{{ill|Tamio Tobe|ja|戸部民夫}}, ''Japanese Mythology'', pp. 76–77.</ref>
Ōnamuji’s mother, {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売}}, mourned his death and ascended to {{ill|Takama-ga-hara|ja|高天原}}, pleading with {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami}} to save him. Kamimusubi sent {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ}}, known respectively as Kisagai-hime ('''𧏛'''貝比売)<ref>'''𧏛''' is composed of "討" beneath "虫".</ref> and Umugi-hime (蛤貝比売), who resurrected Ōnamuji through their healing arts.<ref>Tobe, pp. 77–79.</ref>
Upon learning of his survival, the Eighty Gods attempted to kill him again. They cut down a massive tree, split it with wedges, and forced Ōnamuji inside before removing the wedges, crushing him to death. His grieving mother found the tree, split it open, and once again revived him. Fearing for his life, she urged him to flee to {{ill|Oyabiko|ja|大屋毘古神}} of {{ill|Kii Province|ja|木国}}.<ref>Tobe, pp. 79–80.</ref>
When Ōnamuji arrived, the Eighty Gods pursued him, demanding his capture. Oyabiko, however, helped him escape by guiding him through a crevice in the trees and directing him to flee to {{ill|Ne-no-Kuni|ja|根の国}}, the land of the dead, where {{ill|Susanoo|ja|須佐之男命}} resided.<ref>Tobe, p. 81.</ref>
=== Journey to Ne-no-Kuni === At Susanoo’s house in Ne-no-Kuni, Ōnamuji encountered Susanoo’s daughter, {{ill|Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売命}}, and they fell in love at first sight. When she informed her father of the guest, Susanoo scoffed, "He is but an ugly man. He should be called {{ill|Ashihara-shikowo|ja|葦原色許男神}} instead," and confined him in a chamber filled with serpents. Suseribime, however, secretly gifted him a sacred scarf (比礼, ''hihire'') and instructed him to wave it three times if the snakes attacked. By following her advice, he survived the night.<ref>Tobe, pp. 82–84.</ref>
The next night, Susanoo placed him in a chamber filled with centipedes and bees. Again, Suseribime provided him with a protective scarf, ensuring his safety.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
On the following day, Susanoo challenged Ōnamuji to retrieve an arrow he had shot into a vast field. However, once Ōnamuji entered, Susanoo set fire to the field, attempting to burn him alive. Desperate, Ōnamuji was approached by a mouse, which whispered, "The inside is hollow, the outside is narrow." Understanding the riddle, Ōnamuji stomped on the ground and fell into a hidden burrow, avoiding the flames. The mouse then returned the lost arrow to him. Meanwhile, believing him dead, Suseribime began preparations for his funeral. However, when Susanoo checked the field, he found Ōnamuji alive and holding the arrow.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
Satisfied, Susanoo allowed Ōnamuji to enter his house and ordered him to remove lice from his hair. To his horror, the lice were actually centipedes. Cleverly, Ōnamuji chewed up the mukuro nuts (椋の実) given to him by Suseribime and mixed them with red clay, pretending to spit them out. Believing his guest was crushing centipedes in his mouth, Susanoo grew fond of him and eventually dozed off.<ref>Tobe, p. 87.</ref>
Seizing the opportunity, Ōnamuji tied Susanoo’s hair to the pillars of the house, blocked the entrance with a boulder, and fled. He took Susanoo’s {{ill|Ikutachi|ja|生大刀|Ikutachi=Ikutachi|lt=Living Sword}}, {{ill|Living Bow and Arrows|ja|生弓矢}}, and Suseribime’s {{ill|Heavenly Decree Harp|ja|天詔琴}}, carrying Suseribime on his back. However, as they fled, the harp struck a tree, producing a loud sound that woke Susanoo. In his struggle to chase them, he pulled down the pillars with his hair still bound, allowing Ōnamuji to escape.<ref>Tobe, p. 88.</ref>
Susanoo pursued him as far as {{ill|Yomotsu Hirasaka|ja|黄泉比良坂}}, the gateway between the underworld and the living world. There, he stopped and called out to the fleeing Ōnamuji, commanding, "Use the sword and bow I have granted you to drive away the Eighty Gods who defy you. You shall henceforth be named Ōkuninushi, also called {{ill|Utsushikunitama|ja|宇都志国玉神}}, and together with Suseribime, you must build a grand palace and rule over the land." With these words, he bade him farewell.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Ōkuninushi returned to {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province }}, where he used his newfound divine weapons to subdue the Eighty Gods, chasing them into the mountains and the riverbanks. With Suseribime as his wife, he built a great palace with pillars rooted in rock and rafters reaching the heavens, thus beginning his rule and shaping the land.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Upon hearing of his rise, Yagami-hime, fearing the wrath of Suseribime, abandoned the child she had borne with Ōnamuji, leaving it in the fork of a tree before returning to her home.<ref>Tobe, p. 92.</ref>
=== The Marriage Quest of Ōkuninushi === {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神}}, another name for Ōkuninushi, sought to wed {{ill|Nunakawahime|ja|沼河比売}} of {{ill|Koshi Province|ja|高志国}}. He journeyed to her land, where they exchanged love poems. However, his actions enraged Suseribime, who became fiercely jealous. As he fled from Izumo to {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province }}, he sang a farewell song to Suseribime, who, in turn, responded with a song offering him a farewell cup. The two exchanged cups, signifying their continued connection, and it is said that they remain enshrined to this day.<ref>Tobe, pp. 93–99.</ref>
== Analysis == * These tales are found in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} but not in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. * The myths demonstrate parallels to initiation rites and worldwide mythological motifs, such as the hero's trials and resurrection. * The healing process involving {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ}} resembles the use of [[:en:Anadara_broughtonii|ark shell]] powder mixed with [[:en:Meretrix_lusoria|clam]] juice for burn treatment, which aligns with traditional Japanese medicine.<ref>富士川游, 中外医事新報, 1915.</ref> * Nunakawahime's name reflects a connection to [[Echigo Province]]’s [[Kubiki District]], where the {{ill|Nunakawa Shrine|ja|奴奈川神社}} is dedicated to her.
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Akaiwa Shrine|ja|赤猪岩神社}} (Tottori Prefecture) – Associated with the rock used to kill Ōnamuji.
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=民夫 戸部|title=日本神話: 神々の壮麗なるドラマ|publisher=[[新紀元社]]|year=2003|isbn=9784775302033}}
== External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.ceres.dti.ne.jp/~alex-x/wakan/kojikix1.html |title=古事記 上巻 |date=19990828030812}} == See Also == * {{ill|Joseph (son of Jacob)|ja|ヨセフ (ヤコブの子)}}
{{Japanese mythology}} {{citation needed|Okuninushi’s myths}}

Believing his guest was crushing centipedes in his mouth, Susanoo grew fond of him and eventually dozed off.<ref>Tobe, p. 87.</ref>
Seizing the opportunity, Ōnamuji tied Susanoo’s hair to the pillars of the house, blocked the entrance with a boulder, and fled. He took Susanoo’s {{ill|Ikutachi|ja|生大刀|Ikutachi=Ikutachi|lt=Living Sword|12=draft}}, {{ill|Living Bow and Arrows|ja|生弓矢|12=draft}}, and Suseribime’s {{ill|Heavenly Decree Harp|ja|天詔琴|12=draft}}, carrying Suseribime on his back. However, as they fled, the harp struck a tree, producing a loud sound that woke Susanoo. In his struggle to chase them, he pulled down the pillars with his hair still bound, allowing Ōnamuji to escape.<ref>Tobe, p. 88.</ref>
Susanoo pursued him as far as {{ill|Yomotsu Hirasaka|ja|黄泉比良坂|12=draft}}, the gateway between the underworld and the living world. There, he stopped and called out to the fleeing Ōnamuji, commanding, "Use the sword and bow I have granted you to drive away the Eighty Gods who defy you. You shall henceforth be named Ōkuninushi, also called {{ill|Utsushikunitama|ja|宇都志国玉神|12=draft}}, and together with Suseribime, you must build a grand palace and rule over the land." With these words, he bade him farewell.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Ōkuninushi returned to {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=draft}}, where he used his newfound divine weapons to subdue the Eighty Gods, chasing them into the mountains and the riverbanks. With Suseribime as his wife, he built a great palace with pillars rooted in rock and rafters reaching the heavens, thus beginning his rule and shaping the land.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Upon hearing of his rise, Yagami-hime, fearing the wrath of Suseribime, abandoned the child she had borne with Ōnamuji, leaving it in the fork of a tree before returning to her home.<ref>Tobe, p. 92.</ref>
=== The Marriage Quest of Ōkuninushi === {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|12=draft}}, another name for Ōkuninushi, sought to wed {{ill|Nunakawahime|ja|沼河比売|12=draft}} of {{ill|Koshi Province|ja|高志国|12=draft}}. He journeyed to her land, where they exchanged love poems. However, his actions enraged Suseribime, who became fiercely jealous. As he fled from Izumo to {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}}, he sang a farewell song to Suseribime, who, in turn, responded with a song offering him a farewell cup. The two exchanged cups, signifying their continued connection, and it is said that they remain enshrined to this day.<ref>Tobe, pp. 93–99.</ref>
== Analysis == * These tales are found in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀|12=draft}} but not in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}. * The myths demonstrate parallels to initiation rites and worldwide mythological motifs, such as the hero's trials and resurrection. * The healing process involving {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|12=draft}} resembles the use of [[:en:Anadara_broughtonii|ark shell]] powder mixed with [[:en:Meretrix_lusoria|clam]] juice for burn treatment, which aligns with traditional Japanese medicine.<ref>富士川游, 中外医事新報, 1915.</ref> * Nunakawahime's name reflects a connection to [[Echigo Province]]’s [[Kubiki District]], where the {{ill|Nunakawa Shrine|ja|奴奈川神社|12=draft}} is dedicated to her.
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Akaiwa Shrine|ja|赤猪岩神社|12=draft}} (Tottori Prefecture) – Associated with the rock used to kill Ōnamuji.
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=民夫 戸部|title=日本神話: 神々の壮麗なるドラマ|publisher=[[新紀元社]]|year=2003|isbn=9784775302033}}

revision.text:
'''Ōkuninushi Mythology''' (大国主の神話) recounts the Japanese mythology surrounding [[Ōkuninushi]] following the tale of the [[Hare of Inaba]].
== Synopsis ==
=== Persecution by the Eighty Gods === {{ill|Ōnamuji|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi|12=draft}} (later known as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft}}) was pursued by his elder brothers, the {{ill|Eighty Gods|ja|八十神|1=Yasogami|lt=Yasogami (80 gods)|12=draft}}, who sought to marry {{ill|Yagami-hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime|12=draft}} of {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=draft}}. However, when Yagami-hime declared her intent to wed Ōnamuji instead, the Eighty Gods became enraged and plotted to kill him. They led Ōnamuji to the foothills of a mountain on the border of {{ill|Hōki Province|ja|伯岐国|12=draft}}, telling him, "A red boar dwells in these mountains. We will drive it down, and you must capture it." As Ōnamuji waited, they instead rolled a burning boulder down the mountain, which he attempted to catch, only to be consumed by the flames and perish.<ref>{{ill|Tamio Tobe|ja|戸部民夫|12=draft}}, ''Japanese Mythology'', pp. 76–77.</ref>
Ōnamuji’s mother, {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=draft|12=draft}}, mourned his death and ascended to {{ill|Takama-ga-hara|ja|高天原|12=draft}}, pleading with {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami|12=draft}} to save him. Kamimusubi sent {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|12=draft}}, known respectively as Kisagai-hime ('''𧏛'''貝比売)<ref>'''𧏛''' is composed of "討" beneath "虫".</ref> and Umugi-hime (蛤貝比売), who resurrected Ōnamuji through their healing arts.<ref>Tobe, pp. 77–79.</ref>
Upon learning of his survival, the Eighty Gods attempted to kill him again. They cut down a massive tree, split it with wedges, and forced Ōnamuji inside before removing the wedges, crushing him to death. His grieving mother found the tree, split it open, and once again revived him. Fearing for his life, she urged him to flee to {{ill|Oyabiko|ja|大屋毘古神|12=draft}} of {{ill|Kii Province|ja|木国|12=draft}}.<ref>Tobe, pp. 79–80.</ref>
When Ōnamuji arrived, the Eighty Gods pursued him, demanding his capture. Oyabiko, however, helped him escape by guiding him through a crevice in the trees and directing him to flee to {{ill|Ne-no-Kuni|ja|根の国|12=draft}}, the land of the dead, where {{ill|Susanoo|ja|須佐之男命|12=draft}} resided.<ref>Tobe, p. 81.</ref>
=== Journey to Ne-no-Kuni === At Susanoo’s house in Ne-no-Kuni, Ōnamuji encountered Susanoo’s daughter, {{ill|Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売命|12=draft}}, and they fell in love at first sight. When she informed her father of the guest, Susanoo scoffed, "He is but an ugly man. He should be called {{ill|Ashihara-shikowo|ja|葦原色許男神|12=draft}} instead," and confined him in a chamber filled with serpents. Suseribime, however, secretly gifted him a sacred scarf (比礼, ''hihire'') and instructed him to wave it three times if the snakes attacked. By following her advice, he survived the night.<ref>Tobe, pp. 82–84.</ref>
The next night, Susanoo placed him in a chamber filled with centipedes and bees. Again, Suseribime provided him with a protective scarf, ensuring his safety.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
On the following day, Susanoo challenged Ōnamuji to retrieve an arrow he had shot into a vast field. However, once Ōnamuji entered, Susanoo set fire to the field, attempting to burn him alive. Desperate, Ōnamuji was approached by a mouse, which whispered, "The inside is hollow, the outside is narrow." Understanding the riddle, Ōnamuji stomped on the ground and fell into a hidden burrow, avoiding the flames. The mouse then returned the lost arrow to him. Meanwhile, believing him dead, Suseribime began preparations for his funeral. However, when Susanoo checked the field, he found Ōnamuji alive and holding the arrow.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
Satisfied, Susanoo allowed Ōnamuji to enter his house and ordered him to remove lice from his hair. To his horror, the lice were actually centipedes. Cleverly, Ōnamuji chewed up the mukuro nuts (椋の実) given to him by Suseribime and mixed them with red clay, pretending to spit them out. Believing his guest was crushing centipedes in his mouth, Susanoo grew fond of him and eventually dozed off.<ref>Tobe, p. 87.</ref>
Seizing the opportunity, Ōnamuji tied Susanoo’s hair to the pillars of the house, blocked the entrance with a boulder, and fled. He took Susanoo’s {{ill|Ikutachi|ja|生大刀|Ikutachi=Ikutachi|lt=Living Sword|12=draft}}, {{ill|Living Bow and Arrows|ja|生弓矢|12=draft}}, and Suseribime’s {{ill|Heavenly Decree Harp|ja|天詔琴|12=draft}}, carrying Suseribime on his back. However, as they fled, the harp struck a tree, producing a loud sound that woke Susanoo. In his struggle to chase them, he pulled down the pillars with his hair still bound, allowing Ōnamuji to escape.<ref>Tobe, p. 88.</ref>
Susanoo pursued him as far as {{ill|Yomotsu Hirasaka|ja|黄泉比良坂|12=draft}}, the gateway between the underworld and the living world. There, he stopped and called out to the fleeing Ōnamuji, commanding, "Use the sword and bow I have granted you to drive away the Eighty Gods who defy you. You shall henceforth be named Ōkuninushi, also called {{ill|Utsushikunitama|ja|宇都志国玉神|12=draft}}, and together with Suseribime, you must build a grand palace and rule over the land." With these words, he bade him farewell.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Ōkuninushi returned to {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=draft}}, where he used his newfound divine weapons to subdue the Eighty Gods, chasing them into the mountains and the riverbanks. With Suseribime as his wife, he built a great palace with pillars rooted in rock and rafters reaching the heavens, thus beginning his rule and shaping the land.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Upon hearing of his rise, Yagami-hime, fearing the wrath of Suseribime, abandoned the child she had borne with Ōnamuji, leaving it in the fork of a tree before returning to her home.<ref>Tobe, p. 92.</ref>
=== The Marriage Quest of Ōkuninushi === {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|12=draft}}, another name for Ōkuninushi, sought to wed {{ill|Nunakawahime|ja|沼河比売|12=draft}} of {{ill|Koshi Province|ja|高志国|12=draft}}. He journeyed to her land, where they exchanged love poems. However, his actions enraged Suseribime, who became fiercely jealous. As he fled from Izumo to {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}}, he sang a farewell song to Suseribime, who, in turn, responded with a song offering him a farewell cup. The two exchanged cups, signifying their continued connection, and it is said that they remain enshrined to this day.<ref>Tobe, pp. 93–99.</ref>
== Analysis == * These tales are found in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀|12=draft}} but not in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}. * The myths demonstrate parallels to initiation rites and worldwide mythological motifs, such as the hero's trials and resurrection. * The healing process involving {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|12=draft}} resembles the use of [[:en:Anadara_broughtonii|ark shell]] powder mixed with [[:en:Meretrix_lusoria|clam]] juice for burn treatment, which aligns with traditional Japanese medicine.<ref>富士川游, 中外医事新報, 1915.</ref> * Nunakawahime's name reflects a connection to [[Echigo Province]]’s [[Kubiki District]], where the {{ill|Nunakawa Shrine|ja|奴奈川神社|12=draft}} is dedicated to her.
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Akaiwa Shrine|ja|赤猪岩神社|12=draft}} (Tottori Prefecture) – Associated with the rock used to kill Ōnamuji.
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=民夫 戸部|title=日本神話: 神々の壮麗なるドラマ|publisher=[[新紀元社]]|year=2003|isbn=9784775302033}}
== External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.ceres.dti.ne.jp/~alex-x/wakan/kojikix1.html |title=古事記 上巻 |date=19990828030812}} == See Also == * {{ill|Joseph (son of Jacob)|ja|ヨセフ (ヤコブの子)|12=draft}}

'''Ōkuninushi Mythology''' (大国主の神話) recounts the Japanese mythology surrounding [[Ōkuninushi]] following the tale of the [[Hare of Inaba]].
== Synopsis ==
=== Persecution by the Eighty Gods === {{ill|Ōnamuji|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi|12=draft}} (later known as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft}}) was pursued by his elder brothers, the {{ill|Eighty Gods|ja|八十神|1=Yasogami|lt=Yasogami (80 gods)|12=draft}}, who sought to marry {{ill|Yagami-hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime|12=draft}} of {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=draft}}. However, when Yagami-hime declared her intent to wed Ōnamuji instead, the Eighty Gods became enraged and plotted to kill him. They led Ōnamuji to the foothills of a mountain on the border of {{ill|Hōki Province|ja|伯岐国|12=draft}}, telling him, "A red boar dwells in these mountains. We will drive it down, and you must capture it." As Ōnamuji waited, they instead rolled a burning boulder down the mountain, which he attempted to catch, only to be consumed by the flames and perish.<ref>{{ill|Tamio Tobe|ja|戸部民夫|12=draft}}, ''Japanese Mythology'', pp. 76–77.</ref>
Ōnamuji’s mother, {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=draft|12=draft}}, mourned his death and ascended to {{ill|Takama-ga-hara|ja|高天原|12=draft}}, pleading with {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami|12=draft}} to save him. Kamimusubi sent {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|12=draft}}, known respectively as Kisagai-hime ('''𧏛'''貝比売)<ref>'''𧏛''' is composed of "討" beneath "虫".</ref> and Umugi-hime (蛤貝比売), who resurrected Ōnamuji through their healing arts.<ref>Tobe, pp. 77–79.</ref>
Upon learning of his survival, the Eighty Gods attempted to kill him again. They cut down a massive tree, split it with wedges, and forced Ōnamuji inside before removing the wedges, crushing him to death. His grieving mother found the tree, split it open, and once again revived him. Fearing for his life, she urged him to flee to {{ill|Oyabiko|ja|大屋毘古神|12=draft}} of {{ill|Kii Province|ja|木国|12=draft}}.<ref>Tobe, pp. 79–80.</ref>
When Ōnamuji arrived, the Eighty Gods pursued him, demanding his capture. Oyabiko, however, helped him escape by guiding him through a crevice in the trees and directing him to flee to {{ill|Ne-no-Kuni|ja|根の国|12=draft}}, the land of the dead, where {{ill|Susanoo|ja|須佐之男命|12=draft}} resided.<ref>Tobe, p. 81.</ref>
=== Journey to Ne-no-Kuni === At Susanoo’s house in Ne-no-Kuni, Ōnamuji encountered Susanoo’s daughter, {{ill|Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売命|12=draft}}, and they fell in love at first sight. When she informed her father of the guest, Susanoo scoffed, "He is but an ugly man. He should be called {{ill|Ashihara-shikowo|ja|葦原色許男神|12=draft}} instead," and confined him in a chamber filled with serpents. Suseribime, however, secretly gifted him a sacred scarf (比礼, ''hihire'') and instructed him to wave it three times if the snakes attacked. By following her advice, he survived the night.<ref>Tobe, pp. 82–84.</ref>
The next night, Susanoo placed him in a chamber filled with centipedes and bees. Again, Suseribime provided him with a protective scarf, ensuring his safety.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
On the following day, Susanoo challenged Ōnamuji to retrieve an arrow he had shot into a vast field. However, once Ōnamuji entered, Susanoo set fire to the field, attempting to burn him alive. Desperate, Ōnamuji was approached by a mouse, which whispered, "The inside is hollow, the outside is narrow." Understanding the riddle, Ōnamuji stomped on the ground and fell into a hidden burrow, avoiding the flames. The mouse then returned the lost arrow to him. Meanwhile, believing him dead, Suseribime began preparations for his funeral. However, when Susanoo checked the field, he found Ōnamuji alive and holding the arrow.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
Satisfied, Susanoo allowed Ōnamuji to enter his house and ordered him to remove lice from his hair. To his horror, the lice were actually centipedes. Cleverly, Ōnamuji chewed up the mukuro nuts (椋の実) given to him by Suseribime and mixed them with red clay, pretending to spit them out. Believing his guest was crushing centipedes in his mouth, Susanoo grew fond of him and eventually dozed off.<ref>Tobe, p. 87.</ref>
Seizing the opportunity, Ōnamuji tied Susanoo’s hair to the pillars of the house, blocked the entrance with a boulder, and fled. He took Susanoo’s {{ill|Ikutachi|ja|生大刀|Ikutachi=Ikutachi|lt=Living Sword|12=draft}}, {{ill|Living Bow and Arrows|ja|生弓矢|12=draft}}, and Suseribime’s {{ill|Heavenly Decree Harp|ja|天詔琴|12=draft}}, carrying Suseribime on his back. However, as they fled, the harp struck a tree, producing a loud sound that woke Susanoo. In his struggle to chase them, he pulled down the pillars with his hair still bound, allowing Ōnamuji to escape.<ref>Tobe, p. 88.</ref>
Susanoo pursued him as far as {{ill|Yomotsu Hirasaka|ja|黄泉比良坂|12=draft}}, the gateway between the underworld and the living world. There, he stopped and called out to the fleeing Ōnamuji, commanding, "Use the sword and bow I have granted you to drive away the Eighty Gods who defy you. You shall henceforth be named Ōkuninushi, also called {{ill|Utsushikunitama|ja|宇都志国玉神|12=draft}}, and together with Suseribime, you must build a grand palace and rule over the land." With these words, he bade him farewell.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Ōkuninushi returned to {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=draft}}, where he used his newfound divine weapons to subdue the Eighty Gods, chasing them into the mountains and the riverbanks. With Suseribime as his wife, he built a great palace with pillars rooted in rock and rafters reaching the heavens, thus beginning his rule and shaping the land.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Upon hearing of his rise, Yagami-hime, fearing the wrath of Suseribime, abandoned the child she had borne with Ōnamuji, leaving it in the fork of a tree before returning to her home.<ref>Tobe, p. 92.</ref>
=== The Marriage Quest of Ōkuninushi === {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|12=draft}}, another name for Ōkuninushi, sought to wed {{ill|Nunakawahime|ja|沼河比売|12=draft}} of {{ill|Koshi Province|ja|高志国|12=draft}}. He journeyed to her land, where they exchanged love poems. However, his actions enraged Suseribime, who became fiercely jealous. As he fled from Izumo to {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}}, he sang a farewell song to Suseribime, who, in turn, responded with a song offering him a farewell cup. The two exchanged cups, signifying their continued connection, and it is said that they remain enshrined to this day.<ref>Tobe, pp. 93–99.</ref> == Analysis == * These tales are found in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀|12=draft}} but not in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}. * The myths demonstrate parallels to initiation rites and worldwide mythological motifs, such as the hero's trials and resurrection. * The healing process involving {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|12=draft}} resembles the use of [[:en:Anadara_broughtonii|ark shell]] powder mixed with [[:en:Meretrix_lusoria|clam]] juice for burn treatment, which aligns with traditional Japanese medicine.<ref>富士川游, 中外医事新報, 1915.</ref> * Nunakawahime's name reflects a connection to [[Echigo Province]]’s [[Kubiki District]], where the {{ill|Nunakawa Shrine|ja|奴奈川神社|12=draft}} is dedicated to her.
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Akaiwa Shrine|ja|赤猪岩神社|12=draft}} (Tottori Prefecture) – Associated with the rock used to kill Ōnamuji.
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=民夫 戸部|title=日本神話: 神々の壮麗なるドラマ|publisher=[[新紀元社]]|year=2003|isbn=9784775302033}}
== External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.ceres.dti.ne.jp/~alex-x/wakan/kojikix1.html |title=古事記 上巻 |date=19990828030812}} == See Also == * {{ill|Joseph (son of Jacob)|ja|ヨセフ (ヤコブの子)|12=draft}}

'''Ōkuninushi Mythology''' (大国主の神話) recounts the Japanese mythology surrounding [[Ōkuninushi]] following the tale of the [[Hare of Inaba]].
== Synopsis ==
=== Persecution by the Eighty Gods === {{ill|Ōnamuji|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi|12=draft}} (later known as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft}}) was pursued by his elder brothers, the {{ill|Eighty Gods|ja|八十神|1=Yasogami|lt=Yasogami (80 gods)|12=draft}}, who sought to marry {{ill|Yagami-hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime|12=draft}} of {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=draft}}. However, when Yagami-hime declared her intent to wed Ōnamuji instead, the Eighty Gods became enraged and plotted to kill him. They led Ōnamuji to the foothills of a mountain on the border of {{ill|Hōki Province|ja|伯岐国|12=draft}}, telling him, "A red boar dwells in these mountains. We will drive it down, and you must capture it." As Ōnamuji waited, they instead rolled a burning boulder down the mountain, which he attempted to catch, only to be consumed by the flames and perish.<ref>{{ill|Tamio Tobe|ja|戸部民夫|12=draft}}, ''Japanese Mythology'', pp. 76–77.</ref>
Ōnamuji’s mother, {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=draft|12=draft}}, mourned his death and ascended to {{ill|Takama-ga-hara|ja|高天原|12=draft}}, pleading with {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami|12=draft}} to save him. Kamimusubi sent {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|12=draft}}, known respectively as Kisagai-hime ('''𧏛'''貝比売)<ref>'''𧏛''' is composed of "討" beneath "虫".</ref> and Umugi-hime (蛤貝比売), who resurrected Ōnamuji through their healing arts.<ref>Tobe, pp. 77–79.</ref>
Upon learning of his survival, the Eighty Gods attempted to kill him again. They cut down a massive tree, split it with wedges, and forced Ōnamuji inside before removing the wedges, crushing him to death. His grieving mother found the tree, split it open, and once again revived him. Fearing for his life, she urged him to flee to {{ill|Oyabiko|ja|大屋毘古神|12=draft}} of {{ill|Kii Province|ja|木国|12=draft}}.<ref>Tobe, pp. 79–80.</ref>
When Ōnamuji arrived, the Eighty Gods pursued him, demanding his capture. Oyabiko, however, helped him escape by guiding him through a crevice in the trees and directing him to flee to {{ill|Ne-no-Kuni|ja|根の国|12=draft}}, the land of the dead, where {{ill|Susanoo|ja|須佐之男命|12=draft}} resided.<ref>Tobe, p. 81.</ref>
=== Journey to Ne-no-Kuni === At Susanoo’s house in Ne-no-Kuni, Ōnamuji encountered Susanoo’s daughter, {{ill|Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売命|12=draft}}, and they fell in love at first sight. When she informed her father of the guest, Susanoo scoffed, "He is but an ugly man. He should be called {{ill|Ashihara-shikowo|ja|葦原色許男神|12=draft}} instead," and confined him in a chamber filled with serpents. Suseribime, however, secretly gifted him a sacred scarf (比礼, ''hihire'') and instructed him to wave it three times if the snakes attacked. By following her advice, he survived the night.<ref>Tobe, pp. 82–84.</ref>
The next night, Susanoo placed him in a chamber filled with centipedes and bees. Again, Suseribime provided him with a protective scarf, ensuring his safety.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
On the following day, Susanoo challenged Ōnamuji to retrieve an arrow he had shot into a vast field. However, once Ōnamuji entered, Susanoo set fire to the field, attempting to burn him alive. Desperate, Ōnamuji was approached by a mouse, which whispered, "The inside is hollow, the outside is narrow." Understanding the riddle, Ōnamuji stomped on the ground and fell into a hidden burrow, avoiding the flames. The mouse then returned the lost arrow to him. Meanwhile, believing him dead, Suseribime began preparations for his funeral. However, when Susanoo checked the field, he found Ōnamuji alive and holding the arrow.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
Satisfied, Susanoo allowed Ōnamuji to enter his house and ordered him to remove lice from his hair. To his horror, the lice were actually centipedes. Cleverly, Ōnamuji chewed up the mukuro nuts (椋の実) given to him by Suseribime and mixed them with red clay, pretending to spit them out. Believing his guest was crushing centipedes in his mouth, Susanoo grew fond of him and eventually dozed off.<ref>Tobe, p. 87.</ref>
Seizing the opportunity, Ōnamuji tied Susanoo’s hair to the pillars of the house, blocked the entrance with a boulder, and fled. He took Susanoo’s {{ill|Ikutachi|ja|生大刀|Ikutachi=Ikutachi|lt=Living Sword|12=draft}}, {{ill|Living Bow and Arrows|ja|生弓矢|12=draft}}, and Suseribime’s {{ill|Heavenly Decree Harp|ja|天詔琴|12=draft}}, carrying Suseribime on his back. However, as they fled, the harp struck a tree, producing a loud sound that woke Susanoo. In his struggle to chase them, he pulled down the pillars with his hair still bound, allowing Ōnamuji to escape.<ref>Tobe, p. 88.</ref>
Susanoo pursued him as far as {{ill|Yomotsu Hirasaka|ja|黄泉比良坂|12=draft}}, the gateway between the underworld and the living world. There, he stopped and called out to the fleeing Ōnamuji, commanding, "Use the sword and bow I have granted you to drive away the Eighty Gods who defy you. You shall henceforth be named Ōkuninushi, also called {{ill|Utsushikunitama|ja|宇都志国玉神|12=draft}}, and together with Suseribime, you must build a grand palace and rule over the land." With these words, he bade him farewell.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Ōkuninushi returned to {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=draft}}, where he used his newfound divine weapons to subdue the Eighty Gods, chasing them into the mountains and the riverbanks. He journeyed to her land, where they exchanged love poems. However, his actions enraged Suseribime, who became fiercely jealous. As he fled from Izumo to {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}}, he sang a farewell song to Suseribime, who, in turn, responded with a song offering him a farewell cup. The two exchanged cups, signifying their continued connection, and it is said that they remain enshrined to this day.<ref>Tobe, pp. 93–99.</ref> == Analysis == * These tales are found in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀|12=draft}} but not in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}. * The myths demonstrate parallels to initiation rites and worldwide mythological motifs, such as the hero's trials and resurrection. * The healing process involving {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|12=draft}} resembles the use of [[:en:Anadara_broughtonii|ark shell]] powder mixed with [[:en:Meretrix_lusoria|clam]] juice for burn treatment, which aligns with traditional Japanese medicine.<ref>富士川游, 中外医事新報, 1915.</ref> * Nunakawahime's name reflects a connection to [[Echigo Province]]’s [[Kubiki District]], where the {{ill|Nunakawa Shrine|ja|奴奈川神社|12=draft}} is dedicated to her.
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Akaiwa Shrine|ja|赤猪岩神社|12=draft}} (Tottori Prefecture) – Associated with the rock used to kill Ōnamuji.

He journeyed to her land, where they exchanged love poems. However, his actions enraged Suseribime, who became fiercely jealous. As he fled from Izumo to {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}}, he sang a farewell song to Suseribime, who, in turn, responded with a song offering him a farewell cup. The two exchanged cups, signifying their continued connection, and it is said that they remain enshrined to this day.<ref>Tobe, pp. 93–99.</ref> == Analysis == * These tales are found in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀|12=draft}} but not in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}. * The myths demonstrate parallels to initiation rites and worldwide mythological motifs, such as the hero's trials and resurrection. * The healing process involving {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|12=draft}} resembles the use of [[:en:Anadara_broughtonii|ark shell]] powder mixed with [[:en:Meretrix_lusoria|clam]] juice for burn treatment, which aligns with traditional Japanese medicine.<ref>富士川游, 中外医事新報, 1915.</ref> * Nunakawahime's name reflects a connection to [[Echigo Province]]’s [[Kubiki District]], where the {{ill|Nunakawa Shrine|ja|奴奈川神社|12=draft}} is dedicated to her.
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Akaiwa Shrine|ja|赤猪岩神社|12=draft}} (Tottori Prefecture) – Associated with the rock used to kill Ōnamuji.

{{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Shinto deities]] [[Category:Shinto in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ōkuninushi’s myths]] [[Category:Ōkuninushi’s mythology]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:大国主の神話]] [[sn:Myth of Ōkuninushi]] [[en:Myth of Ōkuninushi]] [[sn:yth of Ōkuninushi]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Myth of Ōkuninushi}}

'''Ōkuninushi Mythology''' (大国主の神話) recounts the Japanese mythology surrounding [[Ōkuninushi]] following the tale of the [[Hare of Inaba]].
== Synopsis ==
=== Persecution by the Eighty Gods === {{ill|Ōnamuji|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi|12=draft}} (later known as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft}}) was pursued by his elder brothers, the {{ill|Eighty Gods|ja|八十神|1=Yasogami|lt=Yasogami (80 gods)|12=draft}}, who sought to marry {{ill|Yagami-hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime|12=draft}} of {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=draft}}. However, when Yagami-hime declared her intent to wed Ōnamuji instead, the Eighty Gods became enraged and plotted to kill him. They led Ōnamuji to the foothills of a mountain on the border of {{ill|Hōki Province|ja|伯岐国|12=draft}}, telling him, "A red boar dwells in these mountains. We will drive it down, and you must capture it." As Ōnamuji waited, they instead rolled a burning boulder down the mountain, which he attempted to catch, only to be consumed by the flames and perish.<ref>{{ill|Tamio Tobe|ja|戸部民夫|12=draft}}, ''Japanese Mythology'', pp. 76–77.</ref>
Ōnamuji’s mother, {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=draft|12=draft}}, mourned his death and ascended to {{ill|Takama-ga-hara|ja|高天原|12=draft}}, pleading with {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami|12=draft}} to save him. Kamimusubi sent {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|12=draft}}, known respectively as Kisagai-hime ('''𧏛'''貝比売)<ref>'''𧏛''' is composed of "討" beneath "虫".</ref> and Umugi-hime (蛤貝比売), who resurrected Ōnamuji through their healing arts.<ref>Tobe, pp. 77–79.</ref>
Upon learning of his survival, the Eighty Gods attempted to kill him again. They cut down a massive tree, split it with wedges, and forced Ōnamuji inside before removing the wedges, crushing him to death. His grieving mother found the tree, split it open, and once again revived him. Fearing for his life, she urged him to flee to {{ill|Oyabiko|ja|大屋毘古神|12=draft}} of {{ill|Kii Province|ja|木国|12=draft}}.<ref>Tobe, pp. 79–80.</ref>
When Ōnamuji arrived, the Eighty Gods pursued him, demanding his capture. Oyabiko, however, helped him escape by guiding him through a crevice in the trees and directing him to flee to {{ill|Ne-no-Kuni|ja|根の国|12=draft}}, the land of the dead, where {{ill|Susanoo|ja|須佐之男命|12=draft}} resided.<ref>Tobe, p. 81.</ref>
=== Journey to Ne-no-Kuni === At Susanoo’s house in Ne-no-Kuni, Ōnamuji encountered Susanoo’s daughter, {{ill|Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売命|12=draft}}, and they fell in love at first sight. When she informed her father of the guest, Susanoo scoffed, "He is but an ugly man. He should be called {{ill|Ashihara-shikowo|ja|葦原色許男神|12=draft}} instead," and confined him in a chamber filled with serpents. Suseribime, however, secretly gifted him a sacred scarf (比礼, ''hihire'') and instructed him to wave it three times if the snakes attacked. By following her advice, he survived the night.<ref>Tobe, pp. 82–84.</ref>
The next night, Susanoo placed him in a chamber filled with centipedes and bees. Again, Suseribime provided him with a protective scarf, ensuring his safety.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
On the following day, Susanoo challenged Ōnamuji to retrieve an arrow he had shot into a vast field. However, once Ōnamuji entered, Susanoo set fire to the field, attempting to burn him alive. Desperate, Ōnamuji was approached by a mouse, which whispered, "The inside is hollow, the outside is narrow." Understanding the riddle, Ōnamuji stomped on the ground and fell into a hidden burrow, avoiding the flames. The mouse then returned the lost arrow to him. Meanwhile, believing him dead, Suseribime began preparations for his funeral. However, when Susanoo checked the field, he found Ōnamuji alive and holding the arrow.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
Satisfied, Susanoo allowed Ōnamuji to enter his house and ordered him to remove lice from his hair. To his horror, the lice were actually centipedes. Cleverly, Ōnamuji chewed up the mukuro nuts (椋の実) given to him by Suseribime and mixed them with red clay, pretending to spit them out. Believing his guest was crushing centipedes in his mouth, Susanoo grew fond of him and eventually dozed off.<ref>Tobe, p. 87.</ref>
Seizing the opportunity, Ōnamuji tied Susanoo’s hair to the pillars of the house, blocked the entrance with a boulder, and fled. He took Susanoo’s {{ill|Ikutachi|ja|生大刀|Ikutachi=Ikutachi|lt=Living Sword|12=draft}}, {{ill|Living Bow and Arrows|ja|生弓矢|12=draft}}, and Suseribime’s {{ill|Heavenly Decree Harp|ja|天詔琴|12=draft}}, carrying Suseribime on his back. However, as they fled, the harp struck a tree, producing a loud sound that woke Susanoo. In his struggle to chase them, he pulled down the pillars with his hair still bound, allowing Ōnamuji to escape.<ref>Tobe, p. 88.</ref>
Susanoo pursued him as far as {{ill|Yomotsu Hirasaka|ja|黄泉比良坂|12=draft}}, the gateway between the underworld and the living world. There, he stopped and called out to the fleeing Ōnamuji, commanding, "Use the sword and bow I have granted you to drive away the Eighty Gods who defy you. You shall henceforth be named Ōkuninushi, also called {{ill|Utsushikunitama|ja|宇都志国玉神|12=draft}}, and together with Suseribime, you must build a grand palace and rule over the land." With these words, he bade him farewell.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Ōkuninushi returned to {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=draft}}, where he used his newfound divine weapons to subdue the Eighty Gods, chasing them into the mountains and the riverbanks. With Suseribime as his wife, he built a great palace with pillars rooted in rock and rafters reaching the heavens, thus beginning his rule and shaping the land.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Upon hearing of his rise, Yagami-hime, fearing the wrath of Suseribime, abandoned the child she had borne with Ōnamuji, leaving it in the fork of a tree before returning to her home.<ref>Tobe, p. 92.</ref>
=== The Marriage Quest of Ōkuninushi === {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|12=draft}}, another name for Ōkuninushi, sought to wed {{ill|Nunakawahime|ja|沼河比売|12=draft}} of {{ill|Koshi Province|ja|高志国|12=draft}}. He journeyed to her land, where they exchanged love poems. However, his actions enraged Suseribime, who became fiercely jealous. As he fled from Izumo to {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}}, he sang a farewell song to Suseribime, who, in turn, responded with a song offering him a farewell cup. The two exchanged cups, signifying their continued connection, and it is said that they remain enshrined to this day.<ref>Tobe, pp. 93–99.</ref> == Analysis == * These tales are found in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀|12=draft}} but not in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}. * The myths demonstrate parallels to initiation rites and worldwide mythological motifs, such as the hero's trials and resurrection. * The healing process involving {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|12=draft}} resembles the use of [[:en:Anadara_broughtonii|ark shell]] powder mixed with [[:en:Meretrix_lusoria|clam]] juice for burn treatment, which aligns with traditional Japanese medicine.<ref>富士川游, 中外医事新報, 1915.</ref> * Nunakawahime's name reflects a connection to [[Echigo Province]]’s [[Kubiki District]], where the {{ill|Nunakawa Shrine|ja|奴奈川神社|12=draft}} is dedicated to her.
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Akaiwa Shrine|ja|赤猪岩神社|12=draft}} (Tottori Prefecture) – Associated with the rock used to kill Ōnamuji.
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=民夫 戸部|title=日本神話: 神々の壮麗なるドラマ|publisher=[[新紀元社]]|year=2003|isbn=9784775302033}}

== Synopsis ==
=== Persecution by the Eighty Gods === {{ill|Ōnamuji|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi|12=draft|12=draft}} (later known as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft|12=draft}}) was pursued by his elder brothers, the {{ill|Eighty Gods|ja|八十神|1=Yasogami|lt=Yasogami (80 gods)|12=draft|12=draft}}, who sought to marry {{ill|Yagami-hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime|12=draft|12=draft}} of {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=draft|12=draft}}. His grieving mother found the tree, split it open, and once again revived him. Fearing for his life, she urged him to flee to {{ill|Oyabiko|ja|大屋毘古神|12=draft|12=draft}} of {{ill|Kii Province|ja|木国|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>Tobe, pp. 79–80.</ref>

The two exchanged cups, signifying their continued connection, and it is said that they remain enshrined to this day.<ref>Tobe, pp. 93–99.</ref> == Analysis == * These tales are found in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀|12=draft|12=draft}} but not in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}. * The myths demonstrate parallels to initiation rites and worldwide mythological motifs, such as the hero's trials and resurrection. * The healing process involving {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|12=draft|12=draft}} resembles the use of [[:en:Anadara_broughtonii|ark shell]] powder mixed with [[:en:Meretrix_lusoria|clam]] juice for burn treatment, which aligns with traditional Japanese medicine.<ref>富士川游, 中外医事新報, 1915.</ref> * Nunakawahime's name reflects a connection to [[Echigo Province]]’s [[Kubiki District]], where the {{ill|Nunakawa Shrine|ja|奴奈川神社|12=draft|12=draft}} is dedicated to her.

=== Journey to Ne-no-Kuni === At Susanoo’s house in Ne-no-Kuni, Ōnamuji encountered Susanoo’s daughter, {{ill|Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売命|12=draft|12=draft}}, and they fell in love at first sight. When she informed her father of the guest, Susanoo scoffed, "He is but an ugly man. He should be called {{ill|Ashihara-shikowo|ja|葦原色許男神|12=draft|12=draft}} instead," and confined him in a chamber filled with serpents. Suseribime, however, secretly gifted him a sacred scarf (比礼, ''hihire'') and instructed him to wave it three times if the snakes attacked. By following her advice, he survived the night.<ref>Tobe, pp. 82–84.</ref>
The next night, Susanoo placed him in a chamber filled with centipedes and bees. Again, Suseribime provided him with a protective scarf, ensuring his safety.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
On the following day, Susanoo challenged Ōnamuji to retrieve an arrow he had shot into a vast field. However, once Ōnamuji entered, Susanoo set fire to the field, attempting to burn him alive. Desperate, Ōnamuji was approached by a mouse, which whispered, "The inside is hollow, the outside is narrow." Understanding the riddle, Ōnamuji stomped on the ground and fell into a hidden burrow, avoiding the flames. The mouse then returned the lost arrow to him. Meanwhile, believing him dead, Suseribime began preparations for his funeral. However, when Susanoo checked the field, he found Ōnamuji alive and holding the arrow.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
Satisfied, Susanoo allowed Ōnamuji to enter his house and ordered him to remove lice from his hair. To his horror, the lice were actually centipedes. Cleverly, Ōnamuji chewed up the mukuro nuts (椋の実) given to him by Suseribime and mixed them with red clay, pretending to spit them out. Believing his guest was crushing centipedes in his mouth, Susanoo grew fond of him and eventually dozed off.<ref>Tobe, p. 87.</ref>
Seizing the opportunity, Ōnamuji tied Susanoo’s hair to the pillars of the house, blocked the entrance with a boulder, and fled. He took Susanoo’s {{ill|Ikutachi|ja|生大刀|Ikutachi=Ikutachi|lt=Living Sword|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Living Bow and Arrows|ja|生弓矢|12=draft|12=draft}}, and Suseribime’s {{ill|Heavenly Decree Harp|ja|天詔琴|12=draft|12=draft}}, carrying Suseribime on his back. However, as they fled, the harp struck a tree, producing a loud sound that woke Susanoo. In his struggle to chase them, he pulled down the pillars with his hair still bound, allowing Ōnamuji to escape.<ref>Tobe, p. 88.</ref>
Susanoo pursued him as far as {{ill|Yomotsu Hirasaka|ja|黄泉比良坂|12=draft|12=draft}}, the gateway between the underworld and the living world. There, he stopped and called out to the fleeing Ōnamuji, commanding, "Use the sword and bow I have granted you to drive away the Eighty Gods who defy you. You shall henceforth be named Ōkuninushi, also called {{ill|Utsushikunitama|ja|宇都志国玉神|12=draft|12=draft}}, and together with Suseribime, you must build a grand palace and rule over the land." With these words, he bade him farewell.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Ōkuninushi returned to {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=draft|12=draft}}, where he used his newfound divine weapons to subdue the Eighty Gods, chasing them into the mountains and the riverbanks. With Suseribime as his wife, he built a great palace with pillars rooted in rock and rafters reaching the heavens, thus beginning his rule and shaping the land.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Upon hearing of his rise, Yagami-hime, fearing the wrath of Suseribime, abandoned the child she had borne with Ōnamuji, leaving it in the fork of a tree before returning to her home.<ref>Tobe, p. 92.</ref>
=== The Marriage Quest of Ōkuninushi === {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|12=draft|12=draft}}, another name for Ōkuninushi, sought to wed {{ill|Nunakawahime|ja|沼河比売|12=draft|12=draft}} of {{ill|Koshi Province|ja|高志国|12=draft|12=draft}}. He journeyed to her land, where they exchanged love poems. However, his actions enraged Suseribime, who became fiercely jealous. As he fled from Izumo to {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft|12=draft}}, he sang a farewell song to Suseribime, who, in turn, responded with a song offering him a farewell cup. The two exchanged cups, signifying their continued connection, and it is said that they remain enshrined to this day.<ref>Tobe, pp. 93–99.</ref> == Analysis == * These tales are found in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀|12=draft|12=draft}} but not in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}. * The myths demonstrate parallels to initiation rites and worldwide mythological motifs, such as the hero's trials and resurrection. * The healing process involving {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|12=draft|12=draft}} resembles the use of [[:en:Anadara_broughtonii|ark shell]] powder mixed with [[:en:Meretrix_lusoria|clam]] juice for burn treatment, which aligns with traditional Japanese medicine.<ref>富士川游, 中外医事新報, 1915.</ref> * Nunakawahime's name reflects a connection to [[Echigo Province]]’s [[Kubiki District]], where the {{ill|Nunakawa Shrine|ja|奴奈川神社|12=draft|12=draft}} is dedicated to her.
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Akaiwa Shrine|ja|赤猪岩神社|12=draft|12=draft}} (Tottori Prefecture) – Associated with the rock used to kill Ōnamuji.
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=民夫 戸部|title=日本神話: 神々の壮麗なるドラマ|publisher=[[新紀元社]]|year=2003|isbn=9784775302033}}

<!--Myth of Ōkuninushi-->

'''Ōkuninushi Mythology''' (大国主の神話) recounts the Japanese mythology surrounding [[Ōkuninushi]] following the tale of the [[Hare of Inaba]].
== Synopsis ==
=== Persecution by the Eighty Gods === {{ill|Ōnamuji|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi|12=draft|12=draft}} (later known as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft|12=draft}}) was pursued by his elder brothers, the {{ill|Eighty Gods|ja|八十神|1=Yasogami|lt=Yasogami (80 gods)|12=draft|12=draft}}, who sought to marry {{ill|Yagami-hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime|12=draft|12=draft}} of {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=draft|12=draft}}. However, when Yagami-hime declared her intent to wed Ōnamuji instead, the Eighty Gods became enraged and plotted to kill him. They led Ōnamuji to the foothills of a mountain on the border of {{ill|Hōki Province|ja|伯岐国|12=draft|12=draft}}, telling him, "A red boar dwells in these mountains. We will drive it down, and you must capture it." As Ōnamuji waited, they instead rolled a burning boulder down the mountain, which he attempted to catch, only to be consumed by the flames and perish.<ref>{{ill|Tamio Tobe|ja|戸部民夫|12=draft|12=draft}}, ''Japanese Mythology'', pp. 76–77.</ref>
Ōnamuji’s mother, {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=draft}}, mourned his death and ascended to {{ill|Takama-ga-hara|ja|高天原|12=draft|12=draft}}, pleading with {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami|12=draft|12=draft}} to save him. Kamimusubi sent {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|12=draft|12=draft}}, known respectively as Kisagai-hime ('''𧏛'''貝比売)<ref>'''𧏛''' is composed of "討" beneath "虫".</ref> and Umugi-hime (蛤貝比売), who resurrected Ōnamuji through their healing arts.<ref>Tobe, pp. 77–79.</ref>
Upon learning of his survival, the Eighty Gods attempted to kill him again. They cut down a massive tree, split it with wedges, and forced Ōnamuji inside before removing the wedges, crushing him to death. His grieving mother found the tree, split it open, and once again revived him. Fearing for his life, she urged him to flee to {{ill|Oyabiko|ja|大屋毘古神|12=draft|12=draft}} of {{ill|Kii Province|ja|木国|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>Tobe, pp. 79–80.</ref>
When Ōnamuji arrived, the Eighty Gods pursued him, demanding his capture. Oyabiko, however, helped him escape by guiding him through a crevice in the trees and directing him to flee to {{ill|Ne-no-Kuni|ja|根の国|12=draft|12=draft}}, the land of the dead, where {{ill|Susanoo|ja|須佐之男命|12=draft|12=draft}} resided.<ref>Tobe, p. 81.</ref>
=== Journey to Ne-no-Kuni === At Susanoo’s house in Ne-no-Kuni, Ōnamuji encountered Susanoo’s daughter, {{ill|Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売命|12=draft|12=draft}}, and they fell in love at first sight. When she informed her father of the guest, Susanoo scoffed, "He is but an ugly man. He should be called {{ill|Ashihara-shikowo|ja|葦原色許男神|12=draft|12=draft}} instead," and confined him in a chamber filled with serpents. Suseribime, however, secretly gifted him a sacred scarf (比礼, ''hihire'') and instructed him to wave it three times if the snakes attacked. By following her advice, he survived the night.<ref>Tobe, pp. 82–84.</ref>
The next night, Susanoo placed him in a chamber filled with centipedes and bees. Again, Suseribime provided him with a protective scarf, ensuring his safety.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
On the following day, Susanoo challenged Ōnamuji to retrieve an arrow he had shot into a vast field. However, once Ōnamuji entered, Susanoo set fire to the field, attempting to burn him alive. Desperate, Ōnamuji was approached by a mouse, which whispered, "The inside is hollow, the outside is narrow." Understanding the riddle, Ōnamuji stomped on the ground and fell into a hidden burrow, avoiding the flames. The mouse then returned the lost arrow to him. Meanwhile, believing him dead, Suseribime began preparations for his funeral. However, when Susanoo checked the field, he found Ōnamuji alive and holding the arrow.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
Satisfied, Susanoo allowed Ōnamuji to enter his house and ordered him to remove lice from his hair. To his horror, the lice were actually centipedes. Cleverly, Ōnamuji chewed up the mukuro nuts (椋の実) given to him by Suseribime and mixed them with red clay, pretending to spit them out. Believing his guest was crushing centipedes in his mouth, Susanoo grew fond of him and eventually dozed off.<ref>Tobe, p. 87.</ref>
Seizing the opportunity, Ōnamuji tied Susanoo’s hair to the pillars of the house, blocked the entrance with a boulder, and fled. He took Susanoo’s {{ill|Ikutachi|ja|生大刀|Ikutachi=Ikutachi|lt=Living Sword|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Living Bow and Arrows|ja|生弓矢|12=draft|12=draft}}, and Suseribime’s {{ill|Heavenly Decree Harp|ja|天詔琴|12=draft|12=draft}}, carrying Suseribime on his back. However, as they fled, the harp struck a tree, producing a loud sound that woke Susanoo. In his struggle to chase them, he pulled down the pillars with his hair still bound, allowing Ōnamuji to escape.<ref>Tobe, p. 88.</ref>
Susanoo pursued him as far as {{ill|Yomotsu Hirasaka|ja|黄泉比良坂|12=draft|12=draft}}, the gateway between the underworld and the living world. There, he stopped and called out to the fleeing Ōnamuji, commanding, "Use the sword and bow I have granted you to drive away the Eighty Gods who defy you. You shall henceforth be named Ōkuninushi, also called {{ill|Utsushikunitama|ja|宇都志国玉神|12=draft|12=draft}}, and together with Suseribime, you must build a grand palace and rule over the land." With these words, he bade him farewell.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Ōkuninushi returned to {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=draft|12=draft}}, where he used his newfound divine weapons to subdue the Eighty Gods, chasing them into the mountains and the riverbanks. With Suseribime as his wife, he built a great palace with pillars rooted in rock and rafters reaching the heavens, thus beginning his rule and shaping the land.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Upon hearing of his rise, Yagami-hime, fearing the wrath of Suseribime, abandoned the child she had borne with Ōnamuji, leaving it in the fork of a tree before returning to her home.<ref>Tobe, p. 92.</ref>
=== The Marriage Quest of Ōkuninushi === {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|12=draft|12=draft}}, another name for Ōkuninushi, sought to wed {{ill|Nunakawahime|ja|沼河比売|12=draft|12=draft}} of {{ill|Koshi Province|ja|高志国|12=draft|12=draft}}. He journeyed to her land, where they exchanged love poems. However, his actions enraged Suseribime, who became fiercely jealous. As he fled from Izumo to {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft|12=draft}}, he sang a farewell song to Suseribime, who, in turn, responded with a song offering him a farewell cup. The two exchanged cups, signifying their continued connection, and it is said that they remain enshrined to this day.<ref>Tobe, pp. 93–99.</ref> == Analysis == * These tales are found in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀|12=draft|12=draft}} but not in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}. * The myths demonstrate parallels to initiation rites and worldwide mythological motifs, such as the hero's trials and resurrection. * The healing process involving {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|12=draft|12=draft}} resembles the use of [[:en:Anadara_broughtonii|ark shell]] powder mixed with [[:en:Meretrix_lusoria|clam]] juice for burn treatment, which aligns with traditional Japanese medicine.<ref>富士川游, 中外医事新報, 1915.</ref> * Nunakawahime's name reflects a connection to [[Echigo Province]]’s [[Kubiki District]], where the {{ill|Nunakawa Shrine|ja|奴奈川神社|12=draft|12=draft}} is dedicated to her.
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Akaiwa Shrine|ja|赤猪岩神社|12=draft|12=draft}} (Tottori Prefecture) – Associated with the rock used to kill Ōnamuji.

revision.text:
'''大国主の神話'''（おおくにぬしのしんわ）では、[[大国主]]についての[[日本神話]]の中で、[[因幡の白兎]]の説話の後のものを記す。
==あらすじ== ===八十神の迫害=== 大穴牟遅神（オオナムヂ、後の大国主）の兄神たちである[[八十神]]（ヤソガミ）は[[因幡国]]の[[八上比売]]（ヤガミヒメ）に求婚するが、ヤガミヒメはオオナムヂと結婚するといったため、八十神はオオナムヂを恨み、殺すことにした。オオナムヂを伯岐国の手前の山麓につれて来て、「赤い猪がこの山にいる。我々が一斉に追い下ろすから、お前は待ち受けてそれを捕えよ」と命令した。オオナムヂが待ち構えていると、八十神は猪に似た大石を火で焼いて転がし落とし、それを捕えようとしたオオナムヂは石の火に焼かれて死んでしまった<ref>[[戸部民夫]] 『日本神話』 76-77頁。</ref>。
オオナムヂの母親の[[刺国若比売]]（サシクニワカヒメ）は息子の死を悲しんで[[高天原]]に上り、[[神産巣日神]]（カミムスビ）に救いを求めた。カミムスビが遣わした[[キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|キサガイヒメ（'''𧏛'''貝比売<ref>'''𧏛'''の字は「討」の下に「虫」である。</ref>）とウムギヒメ（蛤貝比売）]]の治療によりオオナムヂは生き返った<ref>戸部民夫 『日本神話』 77-79頁。</ref>。
オオナムヂの復活を知った八十神は、再度殺害を試みた。大木を切り倒して楔で割れ目を作り、そのなかにオオナムヂを入らせ、楔を引き抜いて打ち殺してしまった。母親は泣きながらオオナムヂを探して大木をみつけ、すぐに木を裂いて取り出して生き返らせた。母親は、「あなたはここにいたら、八十神に滅ぼされてしまうだろう」といい、木国の[[大屋毘古神]]（オオヤビコ）の所へ行かせた<ref>戸部民夫 『日本神話』 79-80頁。</ref>。
オオヤビコの所へ行くと、追ってきた八十神がオオナムヂの引き渡しを求めた。オオヤビコはオオナムヂを木の股を潜り抜けさせて逃がし、[[須佐之男命]]（スサノオ）のいる[[根の国|根の堅州国]]に向かうようにいった<ref>戸部民夫 『日本神話』 81頁。</ref>。
===根の国訪問=== 根の国のスサノオの家で、オオナムヂはスサノオの娘の[[須勢理毘売命]]（スセリビメ）と出会い、二柱は一目惚れした。スセリビメが「とても立派な神が来られました」というので、スサノオはオオナムヂを呼び入れたが「ただの醜男ではないか。葦原色許男神（アシハラシコヲ）と言った方が良い。蛇の室（むろや）にでも泊めてやれ」と、蛇がいる室に寝させた。スセリビメは「蛇の比礼（ひれ：女性が、結ばずに首の左右から前に垂らす[[スカーフ]]の様なもの）」を葦原色許男神（大国主）にさずけ、蛇が食いつこうとしたら比礼を三度振るよういった。その通りにすると蛇は鎮まったので、葦原色許男神は無事に一晩寝て蛇の室を出られた。次の日の夜、スサノオは葦原色許男神を呉公（ムカデ）と蜂がいる室で寝させた。スセリビメは「呉公と蜂の比礼」をさずけたので、葦原色許男神は無事にムカデと蜂の室を出られた<ref>戸部民夫 『日本神話』 82-84頁。</ref>。
スサノオは広い野原の中に射込んだ鳴鏑（なりかぶら）を拾うよう葦原色許男神に命じた。葦原色許男神が野原に入ると、スサノオは火を放って野原を焼き囲んだ。葦原色許男神が困っていると鼠が来て、「内はほらほら、外はすぶすぶ」（穴の内側は広い、穴の入り口はすぼまって狭い）といった。それを理解した葦原色許男神がその場を踏んでみると、地面の中に空いていた穴に落ちて隠れることができ、火をやり過ごせた。また，その鼠はスサノオが射た鳴鏑を咥えて持って来てくれた。スセリビメは葦原色許男神が死んだと思って泣きながら葬式の準備をした。スサノオは葦原色許男神の死を確認しに野原に出てみると、そこに矢を持った葦原色許男神が帰って来た<ref>戸部民夫 『日本神話』 84-87頁。</ref>。
スサノオは葦原色許男神を家に入れ、頭の虱を取るように言った。ところが、その頭にいたのはムカデであった。葦原色許男神は，スセリビメからもらった椋（むく）の実を噛み砕き、同じくヒメにもらった赤土を口に含んで吐き出していると、スサノオはムカデを噛み砕いているのだと思い、かわいい奴だと思いながら眠りに落ちた<ref>戸部民夫 『日本神話』 87頁。</ref>。
葦原色許男神はこの隙に逃げようと思い、スサノオの髪を部屋の柱に結びつけ、大きな石で部屋の入口を塞いだ。スサノオの[[生大刀]]と[[生弓矢]]、スセリビメの[[天詔琴]]を持ち、スセリビメを背負って逃げ出そうとした時、琴が木に触れて鳴り響いた。その音でスサノオは目を覚ましたが、その際に髪が結びつけられていた柱を引き倒してしまった。スサノオが柱から髪を解く間に、葦原色許男神は逃げることができた<ref>戸部民夫 『日本神話』 88頁。</ref>。
スサノオは、[[葦原中津国]]（地上）に通じる[[黄泉比良坂]]（よもつひらさか）まで葦原色許男神を追ったが、そこで止まって逃げる葦原色許男神に「お前が持つ大刀と弓矢で従わない八十神を追い払え。そしてお前が大国主、また宇都志国玉神（ウツシクニタマ）になって、スセリビメを妻として立派な宮殿を建てて住め。この野郎め」といった。葦原色許男神は出雲国へ戻って大国主となりスサノオから授かった太刀と弓矢を持って、八十神を山坂の裾に追い伏せ、また河の瀬に追い払い、全て退けた。そしてスセリビメを正妻にして、宇迦の山のふもとの岩の根に宮柱を立て、高天原に届く様な立派な千木（ちぎ）のある新宮を建てて住み、国づくりを始めた<ref>戸部民夫 『日本神話』 90-92頁。</ref>。
ヤガミヒメは本妻のスセリビメを恐れ、[[木俣神|オオナムヂとの間に生んだ子]]を木の俣に刺し挟んで実家に帰った<ref>戸部民夫 『日本神話』 92頁。</ref>。
===大国主の妻問い=== 八千矛神（ヤチホコ、大国主の別名）は高志国の[[沼河比売]]（ヌナカワヒメ）をめとろうと出かけ、歌をよみかわした。そのため、妻のスセリビメが大変嫉妬した。困惑したヤチホコは出雲国から大和国に逃れる際にスセリビメに歌をよむと、スセリビメは杯を捧げて留める歌を返した。二神は杯を交わし、今に至るまで鎮座している<ref>戸部民夫 『日本神話』 93-99頁。</ref>。
==解説== *これらの説話は『[[古事記]]』、『[[先代旧事本紀]]』第4巻 地祇本紀にあるが『[[日本書紀]]』にはない。 *比較神話：八十神の迫害と根の国訪問の説話は[[課題婚]]型と呼ばれる神話形式に該当し、世界各地に類話がみられる。オオナムヂが死んで母親の力で蘇生する話は[[フィンランド]]の叙事詩『[[カレワラ]]』と酷似しているとの指摘がある。これらの話を成人の[[通過儀礼]]を表すとする説もある<ref>戸部民夫 『日本神話』 79頁。</ref>。 *[[鳥取県]][[西伯郡]][[南部町 (鳥取県)|南部町]]にある[[赤猪岩神社]]では、オオナムヂを焼き潰したとされる大岩が祀ってある。 *カミムスビが遣わしたキサガイヒメは[[アカガイ|赤貝]]、ウムギヒメは[[ハマグリ|蛤]]であり、赤貝の殻の粉を蛤汁で溶いて火傷に塗布したと考えられる<ref>富士川游「史談－日本医史：大穴牟遲神」『中外医事新報』1915年、835号、p46-47</ref>。これは後の石灰乳（''Linimentum Calcis''）に通じ、火傷の妥当な治療法であったと考えられる<ref>伊沢凡人ら「中国医学の生薬療法と混同されやすいわが国・固有の生薬療法-和法」『保健の科学』2001年、43巻、8号、p595-596</ref>。 *オオヤビコのいる「木国」（きのくに）は一般に[[紀伊国]]と解され、これを根拠に当時[[出雲国|出雲]]と紀伊とに交流があったとされる。 *ヌナカワヒメという名前は[[越国]][[頸城郡]]沼川郷にちなむ。[[新潟県]][[糸魚川市]]にはヌナカワヒメを祀る[[天津神社 (糸魚川市)#摂末社|奴奈川神社]]がある。
== 脚注 == <references />
==参考文献== *{{Cite book|和書 |author=戸部民夫 |others=[[神谷礼子]]画 |title=日本神話…神々の壮麗なるドラマ |origdate=2003-10-26 |accessdate=2009-12-06 |edition=初版 |publisher=[[新紀元社]] |series=Truth In Fantasy |isbn=9784775302033 }}

==解説== *これらの説話は『[[古事記]]』、『[[先代旧事本紀]]』第4巻 地祇本紀にあるが『[[日本書紀]]』にはない。 *比較神話：八十神の迫害と根の国訪問の説話は[[課題婚]]型と呼ばれる神話形式に該当し、世界各地に類話がみられる。オオナムヂが死んで母親の力で蘇生する話は[[フィンランド]]の叙事詩『[[カレワラ]]』と酷似しているとの指摘がある。これらの話を成人の[[通過儀礼]]を表すとする説もある<ref>戸部民夫 『日本神話』 79頁。</ref>。 *[[鳥取県]][[西伯郡]][[南部町 (鳥取県)|南部町]]にある[[赤猪岩神社]]では、オオナムヂを焼き潰したとされる大岩が祀ってある。 *カミムスビが遣わしたキサガイヒメは[[アカガイ|赤貝]]、ウムギヒメは[[ハマグリ|蛤]]であり、赤貝の殻の粉を蛤汁で溶いて火傷に塗布したと考えられる<ref>富士川游「史談－日本医史：大穴牟遲神」『中外医事新報』1915年、835号、p46-47</ref>。これは後の石灰乳（''Linimentum Calcis''）に通じ、火傷の妥当な治療法であったと考えられる<ref>伊沢凡人ら「中国医学の生薬療法と混同されやすいわが国・固有の生薬療法-和法」『保健の科学』2001年、43巻、8号、p595-596</ref>。 *オオヤビコのいる「木国」（きのくに）は一般に[[紀伊国]]と解され、これを根拠に当時[[出雲国|出雲]]と紀伊とに交流があったとされる。 *ヌナカワヒメという名前は[[越国]][[頸城郡]]沼川郷にちなむ。[[新潟県]][[糸魚川市]]にはヌナカワヒメを祀る[[天津神社 (糸魚川市)#摂末社|奴奈川神社]]がある。
== 脚注 == <references />

== 関連項目 == * [[ヨセフ (ヤコブの子)|ヨセフ]]

'''Ōkuninushi Mythology''' (大国主の神話) recounts the Japanese mythology surrounding [[Ōkuninushi]] following the tale of the [[Hare of Inaba]].
== Synopsis ==
=== Persecution by the Eighty Gods === {{ill|Ōnamuji|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}} (later known as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}) was pursued by his elder brothers, the {{ill|Eighty Gods|ja|八十神|1=Yasogami|lt=Yasogami (80 gods)}}, who sought to marry {{ill|Yagami-hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}} of {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province}}. However, when Yagami-hime declared her intent to wed Ōnamuji instead, the Eighty Gods became enraged and plotted to kill him. They led Ōnamuji to the foothills of a mountain on the border of {{ill|Hōki Province|ja|伯岐国}}, telling him, "A red boar dwells in these mountains. We will drive it down, and you must capture it." As Ōnamuji waited, they instead rolled a burning boulder down the mountain, which he attempted to catch, only to be consumed by the flames and perish.<ref>{{ill|Tamio Tobe|ja|戸部民夫}}, ''Japanese Mythology'', pp. 76–77.</ref>
Ōnamuji’s mother, {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime}}, mourned his death and ascended to {{ill|Takama-ga-hara|ja|高天原}}, pleading with {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami}} to save him. Kamimusubi sent {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ}}, known respectively as Kisagai-hime ('''𧏛'''貝比売)<ref>'''𧏛''' is composed of "討" beneath "虫".</ref> and Umugi-hime (蛤貝比売), who resurrected Ōnamuji through their healing arts.<ref>Tobe, pp. 77–79.</ref>
Upon learning of his survival, the Eighty Gods attempted to kill him again. They cut down a massive tree, split it with wedges, and forced Ōnamuji inside before removing the wedges, crushing him to death. His grieving mother found the tree, split it open, and once again revived him. Fearing for his life, she urged him to flee to {{ill|Oyabiko|ja|大屋毘古神}} of {{ill|Kii Province|ja|木国}}.<ref>Tobe, pp. 79–80.</ref>
When Ōnamuji arrived, the Eighty Gods pursued him, demanding his capture. Oyabiko, however, helped him escape by guiding him through a crevice in the trees and directing him to flee to {{ill|Ne-no-Kuni|ja|根の国}}, the land of the dead, where {{ill|Susanoo|ja|須佐之男命}} resided.<ref>Tobe, p. 81.</ref>
=== Journey to Ne-no-Kuni === At Susanoo’s house in Ne-no-Kuni, Ōnamuji encountered Susanoo’s daughter, {{ill|Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売命}}, and they fell in love at first sight. When she informed her father of the guest, Susanoo scoffed, "He is but an ugly man. He should be called {{ill|Ashihara-shikowo|ja|葦原色許男神}} instead," and confined him in a chamber filled with serpents. Suseribime, however, secretly gifted him a sacred scarf (比礼, ''hihire'') and instructed him to wave it three times if the snakes attacked. By following her advice, he survived the night.<ref>Tobe, pp. 82–84.</ref>
The next night, Susanoo placed him in a chamber filled with centipedes and bees. Again, Suseribime provided him with a protective scarf, ensuring his safety.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
On the following day, Susanoo challenged Ōnamuji to retrieve an arrow he had shot into a vast field. However, once Ōnamuji entered, Susanoo set fire to the field, attempting to burn him alive. Desperate, Ōnamuji was approached by a mouse, which whispered, "The inside is hollow, the outside is narrow." Understanding the riddle, Ōnamuji stomped on the ground and fell into a hidden burrow, avoiding the flames. The mouse then returned the lost arrow to him. Meanwhile, believing him dead, Suseribime began preparations for his funeral. However, when Susanoo checked the field, he found Ōnamuji alive and holding the arrow.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
Satisfied, Susanoo allowed Ōnamuji to enter his house and ordered him to remove lice from his hair. To his horror, the lice were actually centipedes. Cleverly, Ōnamuji chewed up the mukuro nuts (椋の実) given to him by Suseribime and mixed them with red clay, pretending to spit them out. Believing his guest was crushing centipedes in his mouth, Susanoo grew fond of him and eventually dozed off.<ref>Tobe, p. 87.</ref>
Seizing the opportunity, Ōnamuji tied Susanoo’s hair to the pillars of the house, blocked the entrance with a boulder, and fled. He took Susanoo’s {{ill|Ikutachi|ja|生大刀|Ikutachi=Ikutachi|lt=Living Sword}}, {{ill|Living Bow and Arrows|ja|生弓矢}}, and Suseribime’s {{ill|Heavenly Decree Harp|ja|天詔琴}}, carrying Suseribime on his back. However, as they fled, the harp struck a tree, producing a loud sound that woke Susanoo. In his struggle to chase them, he pulled down the pillars with his hair still bound, allowing Ōnamuji to escape.<ref>Tobe, p. 88.</ref>
Susanoo pursued him as far as {{ill|Yomotsu Hirasaka|ja|黄泉比良坂}}, the gateway between the underworld and the living world. There, he stopped and called out to the fleeing Ōnamuji, commanding, "Use the sword and bow I have granted you to drive away the Eighty Gods who defy you. You shall henceforth be named Ōkuninushi, also called {{ill|Utsushikunitama|ja|宇都志国玉神}}, and together with Suseribime, you must build a grand palace and rule over the land." With these words, he bade him farewell.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Ōkuninushi returned to {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province}}, where he used his newfound divine weapons to subdue the Eighty Gods, chasing them into the mountains and the riverbanks. With Suseribime as his wife, he built a great palace with pillars rooted in rock and rafters reaching the heavens, thus beginning his rule and shaping the land.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Upon hearing of his rise, Yagami-hime, fearing the wrath of Suseribime, abandoned the child she had borne with Ōnamuji, leaving it in the fork of a tree before returning to her home.<ref>Tobe, p. 92.</ref>
=== The Marriage Quest of Ōkuninushi === {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神}}, another name for Ōkuninushi, sought to wed {{ill|Nunakawahime|ja|沼河比売}} of {{ill|Koshi Province|ja|高志国}}. He journeyed to her land, where they exchanged love poems. However, his actions enraged Suseribime, who became fiercely jealous. As he fled from Izumo to {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province}}, he sang a farewell song to Suseribime, who, in turn, responded with a song offering him a farewell cup. The two exchanged cups, signifying their continued connection, and it is said that they remain enshrined to this day.<ref>Tobe, pp. 93–99.</ref> == Analysis == * These tales are found in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} but not in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. * The myths demonstrate parallels to initiation rites and worldwide mythological motifs, such as the hero's trials and resurrection. * The healing process involving {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ}} resembles the use of [[:en:Anadara_broughtonii|ark shell]] powder mixed with [[:en:Meretrix_lusoria|clam]] juice for burn treatment, which aligns with traditional Japanese medicine.<ref>富士川游, 中外医事新報, 1915.</ref> * Nunakawahime's name reflects a connection to [[Echigo Province]]’s [[Kubiki District]], where the {{ill|Nunakawa Shrine|ja|奴奈川神社}} is dedicated to her.
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Akaiwa Shrine|ja|赤猪岩神社}} (Tottori Prefecture) – Associated with the rock used to kill Ōnamuji.
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=民夫 戸部|title=日本神話: 神々の壮麗なるドラマ|publisher=[[新紀元社]]|year=2003|isbn=9784775302033}}
== External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.ceres.dti.ne.jp/~alex-x/wakan/kojikix1.html |title=古事記 上巻 |date=19990828030812}} == See Also == * {{ill|Joseph (son of Jacob)|ja|ヨセフ (ヤコブの子)}}

'''Ōkuninushi Mythology''' (大国主の神話) recounts the Japanese mythology surrounding [[Ōkuninushi]] following the tale of the [[Hare of Inaba]].
== Synopsis ==
=== Persecution by the Eighty Gods === {{ill|Ōnamuji|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}} (later known as [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]]) was pursued by his elder brothers, the {{ill|Eighty Gods|ja|八十神|1=Yasogami|lt=Yasogami (80 gods)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Gods|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasogami|qq=}}, who sought to marry {{ill|Yagami-hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yagami-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Yagami-hime|qq=}} of {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Province|qq=}}. However, when Yagami-hime declared her intent to wed Ōnamuji instead, the Eighty Gods became enraged and plotted to kill him. They led Ōnamuji to the foothills of a mountain on the border of {{ill|Hōki Province|ja|伯岐国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki Province|qq=}}, telling him, "A red boar dwells in these mountains. We will drive it down, and you must capture it." As Ōnamuji waited, they instead rolled a burning boulder down the mountain, which he attempted to catch, only to be consumed by the flames and perish.<ref>{{ill|Tamio Tobe|ja|戸部民夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamio Tobe|qq=}}, ''Japanese Mythology'', pp. 76–77.</ref>
Ōnamuji’s mother, {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, mourned his death and ascended to [[:en:Takamagahara|Takama-ga-hara]], pleading with {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|qq=}} to save him. Kamimusubi sent {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|qq=}}, known respectively as Kisagai-hime ('''𧏛'''貝比売)<ref>'''𧏛''' is composed of "討" beneath "虫".</ref> and Umugi-hime (蛤貝比売), who resurrected Ōnamuji through their healing arts.<ref>Tobe, pp. 77–79.</ref>
Upon learning of his survival, the Eighty Gods attempted to kill him again. They cut down a massive tree, split it with wedges, and forced Ōnamuji inside before removing the wedges, crushing him to death. His grieving mother found the tree, split it open, and once again revived him. Fearing for his life, she urged him to flee to {{ill|Oyabiko|ja|大屋毘古神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyabiko|qq=}} of {{ill|Kii Province|ja|木国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Province|qq=}}.<ref>Tobe, pp. 79–80.</ref>
When Ōnamuji arrived, the Eighty Gods pursued him, demanding his capture. Oyabiko, however, helped him escape by guiding him through a crevice in the trees and directing him to flee to [[:en:Ne-no-kuni|Ne-no-Kuni]], the land of the dead, where {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} resided.<ref>Tobe, p. 81.</ref>
=== Journey to Ne-no-Kuni === At Susanoo’s house in Ne-no-Kuni, Ōnamuji encountered Susanoo’s daughter, {{ill|Suseri-Hime|lt=Suseri-Hime|lt=Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suseribime|qq=}}, and they fell in love at first sight. When she informed her father of the guest, Susanoo scoffed, "He is but an ugly man. He should be called {{ill|Ashihara-shikowo|ja|葦原色許男神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara-shikowo|qq=}} instead," and confined him in a chamber filled with serpents. Suseribime, however, secretly gifted him a sacred scarf (比礼, ''hihire'') and instructed him to wave it three times if the snakes attacked. By following her advice, he survived the night.<ref>Tobe, pp. 82–84.</ref>
The next night, Susanoo placed him in a chamber filled with centipedes and bees. Again, Suseribime provided him with a protective scarf, ensuring his safety.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
On the following day, Susanoo challenged Ōnamuji to retrieve an arrow he had shot into a vast field. However, once Ōnamuji entered, Susanoo set fire to the field, attempting to burn him alive. Desperate, Ōnamuji was approached by a mouse, which whispered, "The inside is hollow, the outside is narrow." Understanding the riddle, Ōnamuji stomped on the ground and fell into a hidden burrow, avoiding the flames. The mouse then returned the lost arrow to him. Meanwhile, believing him dead, Suseribime began preparations for his funeral. However, when Susanoo checked the field, he found Ōnamuji alive and holding the arrow.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
Satisfied, Susanoo allowed Ōnamuji to enter his house and ordered him to remove lice from his hair. To his horror, the lice were actually centipedes. Cleverly, Ōnamuji chewed up the mukuro nuts (椋の実) given to him by Suseribime and mixed them with red clay, pretending to spit them out. Believing his guest was crushing centipedes in his mouth, Susanoo grew fond of him and eventually dozed off.<ref>Tobe, p. 87.</ref>
Seizing the opportunity, Ōnamuji tied Susanoo’s hair to the pillars of the house, blocked the entrance with a boulder, and fled. He took Susanoo’s {{ill|Ikutachi|ja|生大刀|Ikutachi=Ikutachi|lt=Living Sword|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikutachi|qq=}}, {{ill|Living Bow and Arrows|ja|生弓矢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Living Bow and Arrows|qq=}}, and Suseribime’s {{ill|Heavenly Decree Harp|ja|天詔琴|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Decree Harp|qq=}}, carrying Suseribime on his back. However, as they fled, the harp struck a tree, producing a loud sound that woke Susanoo. In his struggle to chase them, he pulled down the pillars with his hair still bound, allowing Ōnamuji to escape.<ref>Tobe, p. 88.</ref>
Susanoo pursued him as far as [[:en:Yomotsu Hirasaka|Yomotsu Hirasaka]], the gateway between the underworld and the living world. There, he stopped and called out to the fleeing Ōnamuji, commanding, "Use the sword and bow I have granted you to drive away the Eighty Gods who defy you. You shall henceforth be named Ōkuninushi, also called {{ill|Utsushikunitama|ja|宇都志国玉神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushikunitama|qq=}}, and together with Suseribime, you must build a grand palace and rule over the land." With these words, he bade him farewell.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Ōkuninushi returned to {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|qq=}}, where he used his newfound divine weapons to subdue the Eighty Gods, chasing them into the mountains and the riverbanks. With Suseribime as his wife, he built a great palace with pillars rooted in rock and rafters reaching the heavens, thus beginning his rule and shaping the land.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Upon hearing of his rise, Yagami-hime, fearing the wrath of Suseribime, abandoned the child she had borne with Ōnamuji, leaving it in the fork of a tree before returning to her home.<ref>Tobe, p. 92.</ref>
=== The Marriage Quest of Ōkuninushi === {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|qq=}}, another name for Ōkuninushi, sought to wed [[:en:Nunakawahime|Nunakawahime]] of {{ill|Koshi Province|ja|高志国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koshi Province|qq=}}. He journeyed to her land, where they exchanged love poems. However, his actions enraged Suseribime, who became fiercely jealous. As he fled from Izumo to [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], he sang a farewell song to Suseribime, who, in turn, responded with a song offering him a farewell cup. The two exchanged cups, signifying their continued connection, and it is said that they remain enshrined to this day.<ref>Tobe, pp. 93–99.</ref> == Analysis == * These tales are found in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]] but not in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. * The myths demonstrate parallels to initiation rites and worldwide mythological motifs, such as the hero's trials and resurrection. * The healing process involving {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|qq=}} resembles the use of [[:en:Anadara_broughtonii|ark shell]] powder mixed with [[:en:Meretrix_lusoria|clam]] juice for burn treatment, which aligns with traditional Japanese medicine.<ref>富士川游, 中外医事新報, 1915.</ref> * Nunakawahime's name reflects a connection to [[Echigo Province]]’s [[Kubiki District]], where the {{ill|Nunakawa Shrine|ja|奴奈川神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunakawa Shrine|qq=}} is dedicated to her.
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Akaiwa Shrine|ja|赤猪岩神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaiwa Shrine|qq=}} (Tottori Prefecture) – Associated with the rock used to kill Ōnamuji.
