'''Ōkuninushi Mythology''' (大国主の神話) recounts the Japanese mythology surrounding [[Ōkuninushi]] following the tale of the [[Hare of Inaba]].
== Synopsis ==
=== Persecution by the Eighty Gods === {{ill|Ōnamuji|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}} (later known as [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]]) was pursued by his elder brothers, the {{ill|Eighty Gods|ja|八十神|1=Yasogami|lt=Yasogami (80 gods)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Gods|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasogami|qq=}}, who sought to marry {{ill|Yagami-hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yagami-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Yagami-hime|qq=}} of {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Province|qq=}}. However, when Yagami-hime declared her intent to wed Ōnamuji instead, the Eighty Gods became enraged and plotted to kill him. They led Ōnamuji to the foothills of a mountain on the border of {{ill|Hōki Province|ja|伯岐国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki Province|qq=}}, telling him, "A red boar dwells in these mountains. We will drive it down, and you must capture it." As Ōnamuji waited, they instead rolled a burning boulder down the mountain, which he attempted to catch, only to be consumed by the flames and perish.<ref>{{ill|Tamio Tobe|ja|戸部民夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamio Tobe|qq=}}, ''Japanese Mythology'', pp. 76–77.</ref>
Ōnamuji’s mother, {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, mourned his death and ascended to [[:en:Takamagahara|Takama-ga-hara]], pleading with {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|qq=}} to save him. Kamimusubi sent {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|qq=}}, known respectively as Kisagai-hime ('''𧏛'''貝比売)<ref>'''𧏛''' is composed of "討" beneath "虫".</ref> and Umugi-hime (蛤貝比売), who resurrected Ōnamuji through their healing arts.<ref>Tobe, pp. 77–79.</ref>
Upon learning of his survival, the Eighty Gods attempted to kill him again. They cut down a massive tree, split it with wedges, and forced Ōnamuji inside before removing the wedges, crushing him to death. His grieving mother found the tree, split it open, and once again revived him. Fearing for his life, she urged him to flee to {{ill|Oyabiko|ja|大屋毘古神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyabiko|qq=}} of {{ill|Kii Province|ja|木国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Province|qq=}}.<ref>Tobe, pp. 79–80.</ref>
When Ōnamuji arrived, the Eighty Gods pursued him, demanding his capture. Oyabiko, however, helped him escape by guiding him through a crevice in the trees and directing him to flee to [[:en:Ne-no-kuni|Ne-no-Kuni]], the land of the dead, where {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} resided.<ref>Tobe, p. 81.</ref>
=== Journey to Ne-no-Kuni === At Susanoo’s house in Ne-no-Kuni, Ōnamuji encountered Susanoo’s daughter, {{ill|Suseri-Hime|lt=Suseri-Hime|lt=Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suseribime|qq=}}, and they fell in love at first sight. When she informed her father of the guest, Susanoo scoffed, "He is but an ugly man. He should be called {{ill|Ashihara-shikowo|ja|葦原色許男神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara-shikowo|qq=}} instead," and confined him in a chamber filled with serpents. Suseribime, however, secretly gifted him a sacred scarf (比礼, ''hihire'') and instructed him to wave it three times if the snakes attacked. By following her advice, he survived the night.<ref>Tobe, pp. 82–84.</ref>
The next night, Susanoo placed him in a chamber filled with centipedes and bees. Again, Suseribime provided him with a protective scarf, ensuring his safety.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
On the following day, Susanoo challenged Ōnamuji to retrieve an arrow he had shot into a vast field. However, once Ōnamuji entered, Susanoo set fire to the field, attempting to burn him alive. Desperate, Ōnamuji was approached by a mouse, which whispered, "The inside is hollow, the outside is narrow." Understanding the riddle, Ōnamuji stomped on the ground and fell into a hidden burrow, avoiding the flames. The mouse then returned the lost arrow to him. Meanwhile, believing him dead, Suseribime began preparations for his funeral. However, when Susanoo checked the field, he found Ōnamuji alive and holding the arrow.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
Satisfied, Susanoo allowed Ōnamuji to enter his house and ordered him to remove lice from his hair. To his horror, the lice were actually centipedes. Cleverly, Ōnamuji chewed up the mukuro nuts (椋の実) given to him by Suseribime and mixed them with red clay, pretending to spit them out. Believing his guest was crushing centipedes in his mouth, Susanoo grew fond of him and eventually dozed off.<ref>Tobe, p. 87.</ref>
Seizing the opportunity, Ōnamuji tied Susanoo’s hair to the pillars of the house, blocked the entrance with a boulder, and fled. He took Susanoo’s {{ill|Ikutachi|ja|生大刀|Ikutachi=Ikutachi|lt=Living Sword|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikutachi|qq=}}, {{ill|Living Bow and Arrows|ja|生弓矢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Living Bow and Arrows|qq=}}, and Suseribime’s {{ill|Heavenly Decree Harp|ja|天詔琴|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Decree Harp|qq=}}, carrying Suseribime on his back. However, as they fled, the harp struck a tree, producing a loud sound that woke Susanoo. In his struggle to chase them, he pulled down the pillars with his hair still bound, allowing Ōnamuji to escape.<ref>Tobe, p. 88.</ref>
Susanoo pursued him as far as [[:en:Yomotsu Hirasaka|Yomotsu Hirasaka]], the gateway between the underworld and the living world. There, he stopped and called out to the fleeing Ōnamuji, commanding, "Use the sword and bow I have granted you to drive away the Eighty Gods who defy you. You shall henceforth be named Ōkuninushi, also called {{ill|Utsushikunitama|ja|宇都志国玉神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushikunitama|qq=}}, and together with Suseribime, you must build a grand palace and rule over the land." With these words, he bade him farewell.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Ōkuninushi returned to {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|qq=}}, where he used his newfound divine weapons to subdue the Eighty Gods, chasing them into the mountains and the riverbanks. With Suseribime as his wife, he built a great palace with pillars rooted in rock and rafters reaching the heavens, thus beginning his rule and shaping the land.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Upon hearing of his rise, Yagami-hime, fearing the wrath of Suseribime, abandoned the child she had borne with Ōnamuji, leaving it in the fork of a tree before returning to her home.<ref>Tobe, p. 92.</ref>
=== The Marriage Quest of Ōkuninushi === {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|qq=}}, another name for Ōkuninushi, sought to wed [[:en:Nunakawahime|Nunakawahime]] of {{ill|Koshi Province|ja|高志国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koshi Province|qq=}}. He journeyed to her land, where they exchanged love poems. However, his actions enraged Suseribime, who became fiercely jealous. As he fled from Izumo to [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], he sang a farewell song to Suseribime, who, in turn, responded with a song offering him a farewell cup. The two exchanged cups, signifying their continued connection, and it is said that they remain enshrined to this day.<ref>Tobe, pp. 93–99.</ref> == Analysis == * These tales are found in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]] but not in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. * The myths demonstrate parallels to initiation rites and worldwide mythological motifs, such as the hero's trials and resurrection. * The healing process involving {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|qq=}} resembles the use of [[:en:Anadara_broughtonii|ark shell]] powder mixed with [[:en:Meretrix_lusoria|clam]] juice for burn treatment, which aligns with traditional Japanese medicine.<ref>富士川游, 中外医事新報, 1915.</ref> * Nunakawahime's name reflects a connection to [[Echigo Province]]’s [[Kubiki District]], where the {{ill|Nunakawa Shrine|ja|奴奈川神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunakawa Shrine|qq=}} is dedicated to her.
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Akaiwa Shrine|ja|赤猪岩神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaiwa Shrine|qq=}} (Tottori Prefecture) – Associated with the rock used to kill Ōnamuji.
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=民夫 戸部|title=日本神話: 神々の壮麗なるドラマ|publisher=[[新紀元社]]|year=2003|isbn=9784775302033}}
== External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.ceres.dti.ne.jp/~alex-x/wakan/kojikix1.html |title=古事記 上巻 |date=19990828030812}} == See Also == * [[:en:Joseph (Genesis)|Joseph (son of Jacob)]]

'''Ōkuninushi Mythology''' (大国主の神話) recounts the Japanese mythology surrounding [[Ōkuninushi]] following the tale of the [[Hare of Inaba]].
== Synopsis ==
=== Persecution by the Eighty Gods === {{ill|Ōnamuji|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}} (later known as [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]]) was pursued by his elder brothers, the {{ill|Eighty Gods|ja|八十神|1=Yasogami|lt=Yasogami (80 gods)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Gods|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasogami|qq=}}, who sought to marry {{ill|Yagami-hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yagami-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Yagami-hime|qq=}} of {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Province|qq=}}. However, when Yagami-hime declared her intent to wed Ōnamuji instead, the Eighty Gods became enraged and plotted to kill him. They led Ōnamuji to the foothills of a mountain on the border of {{ill|Hōki Province|ja|伯岐国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki Province|qq=}}, telling him, "A red boar dwells in these mountains. We will drive it down, and you must capture it." As Ōnamuji waited, they instead rolled a burning boulder down the mountain, which he attempted to catch, only to be consumed by the flames and perish.<ref>{{ill|Tamio Tobe|ja|戸部民夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamio Tobe|qq=}}, ''Japanese Mythology'', pp. 76–77.</ref>
Ōnamuji’s mother, {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, mourned his death and ascended to [[:en:Takamagahara|Takama-ga-hara]], pleading with {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|qq=}} to save him. Kamimusubi sent {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|qq=}}, known respectively as Kisagai-hime ('''𧏛'''貝比売)<ref>'''𧏛''' is composed of "討" beneath "虫".</ref> and Umugi-hime (蛤貝比売), who resurrected Ōnamuji through their healing arts.<ref>Tobe, pp. 77–79.</ref>
Upon learning of his survival, the Eighty Gods attempted to kill him again. They cut down a massive tree, split it with wedges, and forced Ōnamuji inside before removing the wedges, crushing him to death. His grieving mother found the tree, split it open, and once again revived him. Fearing for his life, she urged him to flee to {{ill|Oyabiko|ja|大屋毘古神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyabiko|qq=}} of {{ill|Kii Province|ja|木国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Province|qq=}}.<ref>Tobe, pp. 79–80.</ref>
When Ōnamuji arrived, the Eighty Gods pursued him, demanding his capture. Oyabiko, however, helped him escape by guiding him through a crevice in the trees and directing him to flee to [[:en:Ne-no-kuni|Ne-no-Kuni]], the land of the dead, where {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} resided.<ref>Tobe, p. 81.</ref>
=== Journey to Ne-no-Kuni === At Susanoo’s house in Ne-no-Kuni, Ōnamuji encountered Susanoo’s daughter, {{ill|Suseri-Hime|lt=Suseri-Hime|lt=Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suseribime|qq=}}, and they fell in love at first sight. When she informed her father of the guest, Susanoo scoffed, "He is but an ugly man. He should be called {{ill|Ashihara-shikowo|ja|葦原色許男神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara-shikowo|qq=}} instead," and confined him in a chamber filled with serpents. Suseribime, however, secretly gifted him a sacred scarf (比礼, ''hihire'') and instructed him to wave it three times if the snakes attacked. By following her advice, he survived the night.<ref>Tobe, pp. 82–84.</ref>
The next night, Susanoo placed him in a chamber filled with centipedes and bees. Again, Suseribime provided him with a protective scarf, ensuring his safety.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
On the following day, Susanoo challenged Ōnamuji to retrieve an arrow he had shot into a vast field. However, once Ōnamuji entered, Susanoo set fire to the field, attempting to burn him alive. Desperate, Ōnamuji was approached by a mouse, which whispered, "The inside is hollow, the outside is narrow." Understanding the riddle, Ōnamuji stomped on the ground and fell into a hidden burrow, avoiding the flames. The mouse then returned the lost arrow to him. Meanwhile, believing him dead, Suseribime began preparations for his funeral. However, when Susanoo checked the field, he found Ōnamuji alive and holding the arrow.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
Satisfied, Susanoo allowed Ōnamuji to enter his house and ordered him to remove lice from his hair. To his horror, the lice were actually centipedes. Cleverly, Ōnamuji chewed up the mukuro nuts (椋の実) given to him by Suseribime and mixed them with red clay, pretending to spit them out. Believing his guest was crushing centipedes in his mouth, Susanoo grew fond of him and eventually dozed off.<ref>Tobe, p. 87.</ref>
Seizing the opportunity, Ōnamuji tied Susanoo’s hair to the pillars of the house, blocked the entrance with a boulder, and fled. He took Susanoo’s {{ill|Ikutachi|ja|生大刀|Ikutachi=Ikutachi|lt=Living Sword|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikutachi|qq=}}, {{ill|Living Bow and Arrows|ja|生弓矢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Living Bow and Arrows|qq=}}, and Suseribime’s {{ill|Heavenly Decree Harp|ja|天詔琴|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Decree Harp|qq=}}, carrying Suseribime on his back. However, as they fled, the harp struck a tree, producing a loud sound that woke Susanoo. In his struggle to chase them, he pulled down the pillars with his hair still bound, allowing Ōnamuji to escape.<ref>Tobe, p. 88.</ref>
Susanoo pursued him as far as [[:en:Yomotsu Hirasaka|Yomotsu Hirasaka]], the gateway between the underworld and the living world. There, he stopped and called out to the fleeing Ōnamuji, commanding, "Use the sword and bow I have granted you to drive away the Eighty Gods who defy you. You shall henceforth be named Ōkuninushi, also called {{ill|Utsushikunitama|ja|宇都志国玉神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushikunitama|qq=}}, and together with Suseribime, you must build a grand palace and rule over the land." With these words, he bade him farewell.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Ōkuninushi returned to {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|qq=}}, where he used his newfound divine weapons to subdue the Eighty Gods, chasing them into the mountains and the riverbanks. With Suseribime as his wife, he built a great palace with pillars rooted in rock and rafters reaching the heavens, thus beginning his rule and shaping the land.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Upon hearing of his rise, Yagami-hime, fearing the wrath of Suseribime, abandoned the child she had borne with Ōnamuji, leaving it in the fork of a tree before returning to her home.<ref>Tobe, p. 92.</ref>
=== The Marriage Quest of Ōkuninushi === {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|qq=}}, another name for Ōkuninushi, sought to wed [[:en:Nunakawahime|Nunakawahime]] of {{ill|Koshi Province|ja|高志国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koshi Province|qq=}}. He journeyed to her land, where they exchanged love poems. However, his actions enraged Suseribime, who became fiercely jealous. As he fled from Izumo to [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], he sang a farewell song to Suseribime, who, in turn, responded with a song offering him a farewell cup. The two exchanged cups, signifying their continued connection, and it is said that they remain enshrined to this day.<ref>Tobe, pp. 93–99.</ref> == Analysis == * These tales are found in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]] but not in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. * The myths demonstrate parallels to initiation rites and worldwide mythological motifs, such as the hero's trials and resurrection. * The healing process involving {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|qq=}} resembles the use of [[:en:Anadara_broughtonii|ark shell]] powder mixed with [[:en:Meretrix_lusoria|clam]] juice for burn treatment, which aligns with traditional Japanese medicine.<ref>富士川游, 中外医事新報, 1915.</ref> * Nunakawahime's name reflects a connection to [[Echigo Province]]’s [[Kubiki District]], where the {{ill|Nunakawa Shrine|ja|奴奈川神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunakawa Shrine|qq=}} is dedicated to her.
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Akaiwa Shrine|ja|赤猪岩神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaiwa Shrine|qq=}} (Tottori Prefecture) – Associated with the rock used to kill Ōnamuji.

'''Ōkuninushi Mythology''' (大国主の神話) recounts the Japanese mythology surrounding [[Ōkuninushi]] following the tale of the [[Hare of Inaba]].
== Synopsis ==
=== Persecution by the Eighty Gods === {{ill|Ōnamuji|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} (later known as [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]]) was pursued by his elder brothers, the {{ill|Eighty Gods|ja|八十神|1=Yasogami|lt=Yasogami (80 gods)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Gods|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasogami|qq=}}, who sought to marry {{ill|Yagami-hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yagami-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Yagami-hime|qq=}} of {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, when Yagami-hime declared her intent to wed Ōnamuji instead, the Eighty Gods became enraged and plotted to kill him. They led Ōnamuji to the foothills of a mountain on the border of {{ill|Hōki Province|ja|伯岐国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, telling him, "A red boar dwells in these mountains. We will drive it down, and you must capture it." As Ōnamuji waited, they instead rolled a burning boulder down the mountain, which he attempted to catch, only to be consumed by the flames and perish.<ref>{{ill|Tamio Tobe|ja|戸部民夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamio Tobe|qq=}}, ''Japanese Mythology'', pp. 76–77.</ref>
Ōnamuji’s mother, {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, mourned his death and ascended to [[:en:Takamagahara|Takama-ga-hara]], pleading with {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ}} to save him. Kamimusubi sent {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|qq=}}, known respectively as Kisagai-hime ('''𧏛'''貝比売)<ref>'''𧏛''' is composed of "討" beneath "虫".</ref> and Umugi-hime (蛤貝比売), who resurrected Ōnamuji through their healing arts.<ref>Tobe, pp. 77–79.</ref>
Upon learning of his survival, the Eighty Gods attempted to kill him again. They cut down a massive tree, split it with wedges, and forced Ōnamuji inside before removing the wedges, crushing him to death. His grieving mother found the tree, split it open, and once again revived him. Fearing for his life, she urged him to flee to {{ill|Oyabiko|ja|大屋毘古神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyabiko|qq=}} of {{ill|Kii Province|ja|木国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 紀伊国}}.<ref>Tobe, pp. 79–80.</ref>
When Ōnamuji arrived, the Eighty Gods pursued him, demanding his capture. Oyabiko, however, helped him escape by guiding him through a crevice in the trees and directing him to flee to [[:en:Ne-no-kuni|Ne-no-Kuni]], the land of the dead, where {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} resided.<ref>Tobe, p. 81.</ref>
=== Journey to Ne-no-Kuni === At Susanoo’s house in Ne-no-Kuni, Ōnamuji encountered Susanoo’s daughter, {{ill|Suseri-Hime|lt=Suseri-Hime|lt=Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suseribime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スセリビメ}}, and they fell in love at first sight. When she informed her father of the guest, Susanoo scoffed, "He is but an ugly man. He should be called {{ill|Ashihara-shikowo|ja|葦原色許男神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara-shikowo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} instead," and confined him in a chamber filled with serpents. Suseribime, however, secretly gifted him a sacred scarf (比礼, ''hihire'') and instructed him to wave it three times if the snakes attacked. By following her advice, he survived the night.<ref>Tobe, pp. 82–84.</ref>
The next night, Susanoo placed him in a chamber filled with centipedes and bees. Again, Suseribime provided him with a protective scarf, ensuring his safety.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
On the following day, Susanoo challenged Ōnamuji to retrieve an arrow he had shot into a vast field. However, once Ōnamuji entered, Susanoo set fire to the field, attempting to burn him alive. Desperate, Ōnamuji was approached by a mouse, which whispered, "The inside is hollow, the outside is narrow." Understanding the riddle, Ōnamuji stomped on the ground and fell into a hidden burrow, avoiding the flames. The mouse then returned the lost arrow to him. Meanwhile, believing him dead, Suseribime began preparations for his funeral. However, when Susanoo checked the field, he found Ōnamuji alive and holding the arrow.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
Satisfied, Susanoo allowed Ōnamuji to enter his house and ordered him to remove lice from his hair. To his horror, the lice were actually centipedes. Cleverly, Ōnamuji chewed up the mukuro nuts (椋の実) given to him by Suseribime and mixed them with red clay, pretending to spit them out. Believing his guest was crushing centipedes in his mouth, Susanoo grew fond of him and eventually dozed off.<ref>Tobe, p. 87.</ref>
Seizing the opportunity, Ōnamuji tied Susanoo’s hair to the pillars of the house, blocked the entrance with a boulder, and fled. He took Susanoo’s {{ill|Ikutachi|ja|生大刀|Ikutachi=Ikutachi|lt=Living Sword|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikutachi|qq=}}, {{ill|Living Bow and Arrows|ja|生弓矢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Living Bow and Arrows|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Suseribime’s {{ill|Heavenly Decree Harp|ja|天詔琴|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Decree Harp|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, carrying Suseribime on his back. However, as they fled, the harp struck a tree, producing a loud sound that woke Susanoo. In his struggle to chase them, he pulled down the pillars with his hair still bound, allowing Ōnamuji to escape.<ref>Tobe, p. 88.</ref>
Susanoo pursued him as far as [[:en:Yomotsu Hirasaka|Yomotsu Hirasaka]], the gateway between the underworld and the living world. There, he stopped and called out to the fleeing Ōnamuji, commanding, "Use the sword and bow I have granted you to drive away the Eighty Gods who defy you. You shall henceforth be named Ōkuninushi, also called {{ill|Utsushikunitama|ja|宇都志国玉神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushikunitama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, and together with Suseribime, you must build a grand palace and rule over the land." With these words, he bade him farewell.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Ōkuninushi returned to {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where he used his newfound divine weapons to subdue the Eighty Gods, chasing them into the mountains and the riverbanks. With Suseribime as his wife, he built a great palace with pillars rooted in rock and rafters reaching the heavens, thus beginning his rule and shaping the land.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Upon hearing of his rise, Yagami-hime, fearing the wrath of Suseribime, abandoned the child she had borne with Ōnamuji, leaving it in the fork of a tree before returning to her home.<ref>Tobe, p. 92.</ref>
=== The Marriage Quest of Ōkuninushi === {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, another name for Ōkuninushi, sought to wed [[:en:Nunakawahime|Nunakawahime]] of {{ill|Koshi Province|ja|高志国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koshi Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 越国}}. He journeyed to her land, where they exchanged love poems. However, his actions enraged Suseribime, who became fiercely jealous. As he fled from Izumo to [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], he sang a farewell song to Suseribime, who, in turn, responded with a song offering him a farewell cup. The two exchanged cups, signifying their continued connection, and it is said that they remain enshrined to this day.<ref>Tobe, pp. 93–99.</ref> == Analysis == * These tales are found in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]] but not in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. * The myths demonstrate parallels to initiation rites and worldwide mythological motifs, such as the hero's trials and resurrection. * The healing process involving {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|qq=}} resembles the use of [[:en:Anadara_broughtonii|ark shell]] powder mixed with [[:en:Meretrix_lusoria|clam]] juice for burn treatment, which aligns with traditional Japanese medicine.<ref>富士川游, 中外医事新報, 1915.</ref> * Nunakawahime's name reflects a connection to [[Echigo Province]]’s [[Kubiki District]], where the {{ill|Nunakawa Shrine|ja|奴奈川神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunakawa Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is dedicated to her.
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Akaiwa Shrine|ja|赤猪岩神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaiwa Shrine|qq=}} (Tottori Prefecture) – Associated with the rock used to kill Ōnamuji.
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=民夫 戸部|title=日本神話: 神々の壮麗なるドラマ|publisher=[[新紀元社]]|year=2003|isbn=9784775302033}}
== External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.ceres.dti.ne.jp/~alex-x/wakan/kojikix1.html |title=古事記 上巻 |date=19990828030812}} == See Also == * [[:en:Joseph (Genesis)|Joseph (son of Jacob)]]

revision.text:
'''Ōkuninushi Mythology''' (大国主の神話) recounts the Japanese mythology surrounding [[Ōkuninushi]] following the tale of the [[Hare of Inaba]].
== Synopsis ==
=== Persecution by the Eighty Gods === {{ill|Ōnamuji|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} (later known as [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]]) was pursued by his elder brothers, the {{ill|Eighty Gods|ja|八十神|1=Yasogami|lt=Yasogami (80 gods)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Gods|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasogami|qq=}}, who sought to marry {{ill|Yagami-hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yagami-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Yagami-hime|qq=}} of {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, when Yagami-hime declared her intent to wed Ōnamuji instead, the Eighty Gods became enraged and plotted to kill him. They led Ōnamuji to the foothills of a mountain on the border of {{ill|Hōki Province|ja|伯岐国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, telling him, "A red boar dwells in these mountains. We will drive it down, and you must capture it." As Ōnamuji waited, they instead rolled a burning boulder down the mountain, which he attempted to catch, only to be consumed by the flames and perish.<ref>{{ill|Tamio Tobe|ja|戸部民夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamio Tobe|qq=}}, ''Japanese Mythology'', pp. 76–77.</ref>
Ōnamuji’s mother, {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, mourned his death and ascended to [[:en:Takamagahara|Takama-ga-hara]], pleading with {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ}} to save him. Kamimusubi sent {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|qq=}}, known respectively as Kisagai-hime ('''𧏛'''貝比売)<ref>'''𧏛''' is composed of "討" beneath "虫".</ref> and Umugi-hime (蛤貝比売), who resurrected Ōnamuji through their healing arts.<ref>Tobe, pp. 77–79.</ref>
Upon learning of his survival, the Eighty Gods attempted to kill him again. They cut down a massive tree, split it with wedges, and forced Ōnamuji inside before removing the wedges, crushing him to death. His grieving mother found the tree, split it open, and once again revived him. Fearing for his life, she urged him to flee to {{ill|Oyabiko|ja|大屋毘古神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyabiko|qq=}} of {{ill|Kii Province|ja|木国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 紀伊国}}.<ref>Tobe, pp. 79–80.</ref>
When Ōnamuji arrived, the Eighty Gods pursued him, demanding his capture. Oyabiko, however, helped him escape by guiding him through a crevice in the trees and directing him to flee to [[:en:Ne-no-kuni|Ne-no-Kuni]], the land of the dead, where {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} resided.<ref>Tobe, p. 81.</ref>
=== Journey to Ne-no-Kuni === At Susanoo’s house in Ne-no-Kuni, Ōnamuji encountered Susanoo’s daughter, {{ill|Suseri-Hime|lt=Suseri-Hime|lt=Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suseribime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スセリビメ}}, and they fell in love at first sight. When she informed her father of the guest, Susanoo scoffed, "He is but an ugly man. He should be called {{ill|Ashihara-shikowo|ja|葦原色許男神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara-shikowo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} instead," and confined him in a chamber filled with serpents. Suseribime, however, secretly gifted him a sacred scarf (比礼, ''hihire'') and instructed him to wave it three times if the snakes attacked. By following her advice, he survived the night.<ref>Tobe, pp. 82–84.</ref>
The next night, Susanoo placed him in a chamber filled with centipedes and bees. Again, Suseribime provided him with a protective scarf, ensuring his safety.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
On the following day, Susanoo challenged Ōnamuji to retrieve an arrow he had shot into a vast field. However, once Ōnamuji entered, Susanoo set fire to the field, attempting to burn him alive. Desperate, Ōnamuji was approached by a mouse, which whispered, "The inside is hollow, the outside is narrow." Understanding the riddle, Ōnamuji stomped on the ground and fell into a hidden burrow, avoiding the flames. The mouse then returned the lost arrow to him. Meanwhile, believing him dead, Suseribime began preparations for his funeral. However, when Susanoo checked the field, he found Ōnamuji alive and holding the arrow.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
Satisfied, Susanoo allowed Ōnamuji to enter his house and ordered him to remove lice from his hair. To his horror, the lice were actually centipedes. Cleverly, Ōnamuji chewed up the mukuro nuts (椋の実) given to him by Suseribime and mixed them with red clay, pretending to spit them out. Believing his guest was crushing centipedes in his mouth, Susanoo grew fond of him and eventually dozed off.<ref>Tobe, p. 87.</ref>
Seizing the opportunity, Ōnamuji tied Susanoo’s hair to the pillars of the house, blocked the entrance with a boulder, and fled. He took Susanoo’s {{ill|Ikutachi|ja|生大刀|Ikutachi=Ikutachi|lt=Living Sword|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikutachi|qq=}}, {{ill|Living Bow and Arrows|ja|生弓矢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Living Bow and Arrows|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Suseribime’s {{ill|Heavenly Decree Harp|ja|天詔琴|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Decree Harp|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, carrying Suseribime on his back. However, as they fled, the harp struck a tree, producing a loud sound that woke Susanoo. In his struggle to chase them, he pulled down the pillars with his hair still bound, allowing Ōnamuji to escape.<ref>Tobe, p. 88.</ref>
Susanoo pursued him as far as [[:en:Yomotsu Hirasaka|Yomotsu Hirasaka]], the gateway between the underworld and the living world. There, he stopped and called out to the fleeing Ōnamuji, commanding, "Use the sword and bow I have granted you to drive away the Eighty Gods who defy you. You shall henceforth be named Ōkuninushi, also called {{ill|Utsushikunitama|ja|宇都志国玉神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushikunitama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, and together with Suseribime, you must build a grand palace and rule over the land." With these words, he bade him farewell.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Ōkuninushi returned to {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where he used his newfound divine weapons to subdue the Eighty Gods, chasing them into the mountains and the riverbanks. With Suseribime as his wife, he built a great palace with pillars rooted in rock and rafters reaching the heavens, thus beginning his rule and shaping the land.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Upon hearing of his rise, Yagami-hime, fearing the wrath of Suseribime, abandoned the child she had borne with Ōnamuji, leaving it in the fork of a tree before returning to her home.<ref>Tobe, p. 92.</ref>
=== The Marriage Quest of Ōkuninushi === {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, another name for Ōkuninushi, sought to wed [[:en:Nunakawahime|Nunakawahime]] of {{ill|Koshi Province|ja|高志国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koshi Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 越国}}. He journeyed to her land, where they exchanged love poems. However, his actions enraged Suseribime, who became fiercely jealous. As he fled from Izumo to [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], he sang a farewell song to Suseribime, who, in turn, responded with a song offering him a farewell cup. The two exchanged cups, signifying their continued connection, and it is said that they remain enshrined to this day.<ref>Tobe, pp. 93–99.</ref> == Analysis == * These tales are found in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]] but not in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. * The myths demonstrate parallels to initiation rites and worldwide mythological motifs, such as the hero's trials and resurrection. * The healing process involving {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|qq=}} resembles the use of [[:en:Anadara_broughtonii|ark shell]] powder mixed with [[:en:Meretrix_lusoria|clam]] juice for burn treatment, which aligns with traditional Japanese medicine.<ref>富士川游, 中外医事新報, 1915.</ref> * Nunakawahime's name reflects a connection to [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo Province]]’s [[Kubiki District]], where the {{ill|Nunakawa Shrine|ja|奴奈川神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunakawa Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is dedicated to her.
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Akaiwa Shrine|ja|赤猪岩神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaiwa Shrine|qq=}} (Tottori Prefecture) – Associated with the rock used to kill Ōnamuji.

== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=民夫 戸部|title=日本神話: 神々の壮麗なるドラマ|publisher=[[新紀元社]]|year=2003|isbn=9784775302033}}

The next night, Susanoo placed him in a chamber filled with centipedes and bees. Again, Suseribime provided him with a protective scarf, ensuring his safety.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
On the following day, Susanoo challenged Ōnamuji to retrieve an arrow he had shot into a vast field. However, once Ōnamuji entered, Susanoo set fire to the field, attempting to burn him alive. Desperate, Ōnamuji was approached by a mouse, which whispered, "The inside is hollow, the outside is narrow." Understanding the riddle, Ōnamuji stomped on the ground and fell into a hidden burrow, avoiding the flames. The mouse then returned the lost arrow to him. Meanwhile, believing him dead, Suseribime began preparations for his funeral. However, when Susanoo checked the field, he found Ōnamuji alive and holding the arrow.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
Satisfied, Susanoo allowed Ōnamuji to enter his house and ordered him to remove lice from his hair. To his horror, the lice were actually centipedes. Cleverly, Ōnamuji chewed up the mukuro nuts (椋の実) given to him by Suseribime and mixed them with red clay, pretending to spit them out. Believing his guest was crushing centipedes in his mouth, Susanoo grew fond of him and eventually dozed off.<ref>Tobe, p. 87.</ref>
Seizing the opportunity, Ōnamuji tied Susanoo’s hair to the pillars of the house, blocked the entrance with a boulder, and fled. He took Susanoo’s {{ill|Ikutachi|ja|生大刀|Ikutachi=Ikutachi|lt=Living Sword|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikutachi|qq=}}, {{ill|Living Bow and Arrows|ja|生弓矢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Living Bow and Arrows|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Suseribime’s {{ill|Heavenly Decree Harp|ja|天詔琴|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Decree Harp|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, carrying Suseribime on his back. However, as they fled, the harp struck a tree, producing a loud sound that woke Susanoo. In his struggle to chase them, he pulled down the pillars with his hair still bound, allowing Ōnamuji to escape.<ref>Tobe, p. 88.</ref>
Susanoo pursued him as far as [[:en:Yomotsu Hirasaka|Yomotsu Hirasaka]], the gateway between the underworld and the living world. There, he stopped and called out to the fleeing Ōnamuji, commanding, "Use the sword and bow I have granted you to drive away the Eighty Gods who defy you. You shall henceforth be named Ōkuninushi, also called {{ill|Utsushikunitama|ja|宇都志国玉神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushikunitama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, and together with Suseribime, you must build a grand palace and rule over the land." With these words, he bade him farewell.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Ōkuninushi returned to {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where he used his newfound divine weapons to subdue the Eighty Gods, chasing them into the mountains and the riverbanks. With Suseribime as his wife, he built a great palace with pillars rooted in rock and rafters reaching the heavens, thus beginning his rule and shaping the land.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Upon hearing of his rise, Yagami-hime, fearing the wrath of Suseribime, abandoned the child she had borne with Ōnamuji, leaving it in the fork of a tree before returning to her home.<ref>Tobe, p. 92.</ref>
=== The Marriage Quest of Ōkuninushi === {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, another name for Ōkuninushi, sought to wed [[:en:Nunakawahime|Nunakawahime]] of {{ill|Koshi Province|ja|高志国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koshi Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 越国}}. He journeyed to her land, where they exchanged love poems. However, his actions enraged Suseribime, who became fiercely jealous. As he fled from Izumo to [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], he sang a farewell song to Suseribime, who, in turn, responded with a song offering him a farewell cup. The two exchanged cups, signifying their continued connection, and it is said that they remain enshrined to this day.<ref>Tobe, pp. 93–99.</ref> == Analysis == * These tales are found in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]] but not in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. * The myths demonstrate parallels to initiation rites and worldwide mythological motifs, such as the hero's trials and resurrection. * The healing process involving {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|qq=}} resembles the use of [[:en:Anadara_broughtonii|ark shell]] powder mixed with [[:en:Meretrix_lusoria|clam]] juice for burn treatment, which aligns with traditional Japanese medicine.<ref>富士川游, 中外医事新報, 1915.</ref> * Nunakawahime's name reflects a connection to [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo Province]]’s [[Kubiki District]], where the {{ill|Nunakawa Shrine|ja|奴奈川神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunakawa Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is dedicated to her.
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Akaiwa Shrine|ja|赤猪岩神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaiwa Shrine|qq=}} (Tottori Prefecture) – Associated with the rock used to kill Ōnamuji.
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=民夫 戸部|title=日本神話: 神々の壮麗なるドラマ|publisher=[[新紀元社]]|year=2003|isbn=9784775302033}}

[[sn:yth of Ōkuninushi]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:大國主之神話]]

'''Ōkuninushi Mythology''' (大国主の神話) recounts the Japanese mythology surrounding [[Ōkuninushi]] following the tale of the [[Hare of Inaba]].
== Synopsis ==
=== Persecution by the Eighty Gods === {{ill|Ōnamuji|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} (later known as [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]]) was pursued by his elder brothers, the {{ill|Eighty Gods|ja|八十神|1=Yasogami|lt=Yasogami (80 gods)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Gods|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasogami|qq=}}, who sought to marry {{ill|Yagami-hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yagami-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Yagami-hime|qq=}} of {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, when Yagami-hime declared her intent to wed Ōnamuji instead, the Eighty Gods became enraged and plotted to kill him. They led Ōnamuji to the foothills of a mountain on the border of {{ill|Hōki Province|ja|伯岐国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, telling him, "A red boar dwells in these mountains. We will drive it down, and you must capture it." As Ōnamuji waited, they instead rolled a burning boulder down the mountain, which he attempted to catch, only to be consumed by the flames and perish.<ref>{{ill|Tamio Tobe|ja|戸部民夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamio Tobe|qq=}}, ''Japanese Mythology'', pp. 76–77.</ref>
Ōnamuji’s mother, {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, mourned his death and ascended to [[:en:Takamagahara|Takama-ga-hara]], pleading with {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ}} to save him. Kamimusubi sent {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|qq=}}, known respectively as Kisagai-hime ('''𧏛'''貝比売)<ref>'''𧏛''' is composed of "討" beneath "虫".</ref> and Umugi-hime (蛤貝比売), who resurrected Ōnamuji through their healing arts.<ref>Tobe, pp. 77–79.</ref>
Upon learning of his survival, the Eighty Gods attempted to kill him again. They cut down a massive tree, split it with wedges, and forced Ōnamuji inside before removing the wedges, crushing him to death. His grieving mother found the tree, split it open, and once again revived him. Fearing for his life, she urged him to flee to {{ill|Oyabiko|ja|大屋毘古神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyabiko|qq=}} of {{ill|Kii Province|ja|木国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 紀伊国}}.<ref>Tobe, pp. 79–80.</ref>
When Ōnamuji arrived, the Eighty Gods pursued him, demanding his capture. Oyabiko, however, helped him escape by guiding him through a crevice in the trees and directing him to flee to [[:en:Ne-no-kuni|Ne-no-Kuni]], the land of the dead, where {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} resided.<ref>Tobe, p. 81.</ref>
=== Journey to Ne-no-Kuni === At Susanoo’s house in Ne-no-Kuni, Ōnamuji encountered Susanoo’s daughter, {{ill|Suseri-Hime|lt=Suseri-Hime|lt=Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suseribime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スセリビメ}}, and they fell in love at first sight. When she informed her father of the guest, Susanoo scoffed, "He is but an ugly man. He should be called {{ill|Ashihara-shikowo|ja|葦原色許男神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara-shikowo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} instead," and confined him in a chamber filled with serpents. Suseribime, however, secretly gifted him a sacred scarf (比礼, ''hihire'') and instructed him to wave it three times if the snakes attacked. By following her advice, he survived the night.<ref>Tobe, pp. 82–84.</ref>
The next night, Susanoo placed him in a chamber filled with centipedes and bees. Again, Suseribime provided him with a protective scarf, ensuring his safety.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
On the following day, Susanoo challenged Ōnamuji to retrieve an arrow he had shot into a vast field. However, once Ōnamuji entered, Susanoo set fire to the field, attempting to burn him alive. Desperate, Ōnamuji was approached by a mouse, which whispered, "The inside is hollow, the outside is narrow." Understanding the riddle, Ōnamuji stomped on the ground and fell into a hidden burrow, avoiding the flames. The mouse then returned the lost arrow to him. Meanwhile, believing him dead, Suseribime began preparations for his funeral. However, when Susanoo checked the field, he found Ōnamuji alive and holding the arrow.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
Satisfied, Susanoo allowed Ōnamuji to enter his house and ordered him to remove lice from his hair. To his horror, the lice were actually centipedes. Cleverly, Ōnamuji chewed up the mukuro nuts (椋の実) given to him by Suseribime and mixed them with red clay, pretending to spit them out. Believing his guest was crushing centipedes in his mouth, Susanoo grew fond of him and eventually dozed off.<ref>Tobe, p. 87.</ref>
Seizing the opportunity, Ōnamuji tied Susanoo’s hair to the pillars of the house, blocked the entrance with a boulder, and fled. He took Susanoo’s {{ill|Ikutachi|ja|生大刀|Ikutachi=Ikutachi|lt=Living Sword|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikutachi|qq=}}, {{ill|Living Bow and Arrows|ja|生弓矢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Living Bow and Arrows|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Suseribime’s {{ill|Heavenly Decree Harp|ja|天詔琴|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Decree Harp|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, carrying Suseribime on his back. However, as they fled, the harp struck a tree, producing a loud sound that woke Susanoo. In his struggle to chase them, he pulled down the pillars with his hair still bound, allowing Ōnamuji to escape.<ref>Tobe, p. 88.</ref>
Susanoo pursued him as far as [[:en:Yomotsu Hirasaka|Yomotsu Hirasaka]], the gateway between the underworld and the living world. There, he stopped and called out to the fleeing Ōnamuji, commanding, "Use the sword and bow I have granted you to drive away the Eighty Gods who defy you. You shall henceforth be named Ōkuninushi, also called {{ill|Utsushikunitama|ja|宇都志国玉神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushikunitama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, and together with Suseribime, you must build a grand palace and rule over the land." With these words, he bade him farewell.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Ōkuninushi returned to {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where he used his newfound divine weapons to subdue the Eighty Gods, chasing them into the mountains and the riverbanks. With Suseribime as his wife, he built a great palace with pillars rooted in rock and rafters reaching the heavens, thus beginning his rule and shaping the land.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Upon hearing of his rise, Yagami-hime, fearing the wrath of Suseribime, abandoned the child she had borne with Ōnamuji, leaving it in the fork of a tree before returning to her home.<ref>Tobe, p. 92.</ref>
=== The Marriage Quest of Ōkuninushi === {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, another name for Ōkuninushi, sought to wed [[:en:Nunakawahime|Nunakawahime]] of {{ill|Koshi Province|ja|高志国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koshi Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 越国}}. He journeyed to her land, where they exchanged love poems. However, his actions enraged Suseribime, who became fiercely jealous. As he fled from Izumo to [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], he sang a farewell song to Suseribime, who, in turn, responded with a song offering him a farewell cup. The two exchanged cups, signifying their continued connection, and it is said that they remain enshrined to this day.<ref>Tobe, pp. 93–99.</ref> == Analysis == * These tales are found in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]] but not in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. * The myths demonstrate parallels to initiation rites and worldwide mythological motifs, such as the hero's trials and resurrection. * The healing process involving {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|qq=}} resembles the use of [[:en:Anadara_broughtonii|ark shell]] powder mixed with [[:en:Meretrix_lusoria|clam]] juice for burn treatment, which aligns with traditional Japanese medicine.<ref>富士川游, 中外医事新報, 1915.</ref> * Nunakawahime's name reflects a connection to [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo Province]]’s [[Kubiki District]], where the {{ill|Nunakawa Shrine|ja|奴奈川神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunakawa Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is dedicated to her.
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Akaiwa Shrine|ja|赤猪岩神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaiwa Shrine|qq=}} (Tottori Prefecture) – Associated with the rock used to kill Ōnamuji.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11433656]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ōkuninushi Mythology''' (大国主の神話) recounts the Japanese mythology surrounding [[Ōkuninushi]] following the tale of the [[Hare of Inaba]].
== Synopsis ==
=== Persecution by the Eighty Gods === {{ill|Ōnamuji|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} (later known as [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]]) was pursued by his elder brothers, the {{ill|Eighty Gods|ja|八十神|1=Yasogami|lt=Yasogami (80 gods)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Gods|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasogami|qq=}}, who sought to marry {{ill|Yagami-hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yagami-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Yagami-hime|qq=}} of {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, when Yagami-hime declared her intent to wed Ōnamuji instead, the Eighty Gods became enraged and plotted to kill him. They led Ōnamuji to the foothills of a mountain on the border of {{ill|Hōki Province|ja|伯岐国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, telling him, "A red boar dwells in these mountains. We will drive it down, and you must capture it." As Ōnamuji waited, they instead rolled a burning boulder down the mountain, which he attempted to catch, only to be consumed by the flames and perish.<ref>{{ill|Tamio Tobe|ja|戸部民夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamio Tobe|qq=}}, ''Japanese Mythology'', pp. 76–77.</ref>
Ōnamuji’s mother, {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, mourned his death and ascended to [[:en:Takamagahara|Takama-ga-hara]], pleading with {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ}} to save him. Kamimusubi sent {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|qq=}}, known respectively as Kisagai-hime ('''𧏛'''貝比売)<ref>'''𧏛''' is composed of "討" beneath "虫".</ref> and Umugi-hime (蛤貝比売), who resurrected Ōnamuji through their healing arts.<ref>Tobe, pp. 77–79.</ref>
Upon learning of his survival, the Eighty Gods attempted to kill him again. They cut down a massive tree, split it with wedges, and forced Ōnamuji inside before removing the wedges, crushing him to death. His grieving mother found the tree, split it open, and once again revived him. Fearing for his life, she urged him to flee to {{ill|Oyabiko|ja|大屋毘古神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyabiko|qq=}} of {{ill|Kii Province|ja|木国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 紀伊国}}.<ref>Tobe, pp. 79–80.</ref>
When Ōnamuji arrived, the Eighty Gods pursued him, demanding his capture. === Journey to Ne-no-Kuni === At Susanoo’s house in Ne-no-Kuni, Ōnamuji encountered Susanoo’s daughter, {{ill|Suseri-Hime|lt=Suseri-Hime|lt=Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suseribime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スセリビメ}}, and they fell in love at first sight. When she informed her father of the guest, Susanoo scoffed, "He is but an ugly man. He should be called {{ill|Ashihara-shikowo|ja|葦原色許男神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara-shikowo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} instead," and confined him in a chamber filled with serpents. Suseribime, however, secretly gifted him a sacred scarf (比礼, ''hihire'') and instructed him to wave it three times if the snakes attacked. By following her advice, he survived the night.<ref>Tobe, pp. 82–84.</ref>
The next night, Susanoo placed him in a chamber filled with centipedes and bees. Again, Suseribime provided him with a protective scarf, ensuring his safety.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
On the following day, Susanoo challenged Ōnamuji to retrieve an arrow he had shot into a vast field. However, once Ōnamuji entered, Susanoo set fire to the field, attempting to burn him alive. Desperate, Ōnamuji was approached by a mouse, which whispered, "The inside is hollow, the outside is narrow." Satisfied, Susanoo allowed Ōnamuji to enter his house and ordered him to remove lice from his hair. To his horror, the lice were actually centipedes. Believing his guest was crushing centipedes in his mouth, Susanoo grew fond of him and eventually dozed off.<ref>Tobe, p. 87.</ref>
Seizing the opportunity, Ōnamuji tied Susanoo’s hair to the pillars of the house, blocked the entrance with a boulder, and fled. He took Susanoo’s {{ill|Ikutachi|ja|生大刀|Ikutachi=Ikutachi|lt=Living Sword|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikutachi|qq=}}, {{ill|Living Bow and Arrows|ja|生弓矢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Living Bow and Arrows|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Suseribime’s {{ill|Heavenly Decree Harp|ja|天詔琴|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Decree Harp|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, carrying Suseribime on his back. However, as they fled, the harp struck a tree, producing a loud sound that woke Susanoo. In his struggle to chase them, he pulled down the pillars with his hair still bound, allowing Ōnamuji to escape.<ref>Tobe, p. 88.</ref>
Susanoo pursued him as far as [[:en:Yomotsu Hirasaka|Yomotsu Hirasaka]], the gateway between the underworld and the living world. There, he stopped and called out to the fleeing Ōnamuji, commanding, "Use the sword and bow I have granted you to drive away the Eighty Gods who defy you. You shall henceforth be named Ōkuninushi, also called {{ill|Utsushikunitama|ja|宇都志国玉神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushikunitama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, and together with Suseribime, you must build a grand palace and rule over the land." With these words, he bade him farewell.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Ōkuninushi returned to {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where he used his newfound divine weapons to subdue the Eighty Gods, chasing them into the mountains and the riverbanks. With Suseribime as his wife, he built a great palace with pillars rooted in rock and rafters reaching the heavens, thus beginning his rule and shaping the land.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Upon hearing of his rise, Yagami-hime, fearing the wrath of Suseribime, abandoned the child she had borne with Ōnamuji, leaving it in the fork of a tree before returning to her home.<ref>Tobe, p. 92.</ref>
=== The Marriage Quest of Ōkuninushi === {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, another name for Ōkuninushi, sought to wed [[:en:Nunakawahime|Nunakawahime]] of {{ill|Koshi Province|ja|高志国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koshi Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 越国}}. He journeyed to her land, where they exchanged love poems. However, his actions enraged Suseribime, who became fiercely jealous. As he fled from Izumo to [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], he sang a farewell song to Suseribime, who, in turn, responded with a song offering him a farewell cup. The two exchanged cups, signifying their continued connection, and it is said that they remain enshrined to this day.<ref>Tobe, pp. 93–99.</ref> == Analysis == * These tales are found in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]] but not in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. * The myths demonstrate parallels to initiation rites and worldwide mythological motifs, such as the hero's trials and resurrection. * The healing process involving {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|qq=}} resembles the use of [[:en:Anadara_broughtonii|ark shell]] powder mixed with [[:en:Meretrix_lusoria|clam]] juice for burn treatment, which aligns with traditional Japanese medicine.<ref>富士川游, 中外医事新報, 1915.</ref> * Nunakawahime's name reflects a connection to [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo Province]]’s [[Kubiki District]], where the {{ill|Nunakawa Shrine|ja|奴奈川神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunakawa Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is dedicated to her.
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Akaiwa Shrine|ja|赤猪岩神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaiwa Shrine|qq=}} (Tottori Prefecture) – Associated with the rock used to kill Ōnamuji.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11433656]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ōkuninushi Mythology''' (大国主の神話) recounts the Japanese mythology surrounding [[Ōkuninushi]] following the tale of the [[Hare of Inaba]].
== Synopsis ==
=== Persecution by the Eighty Gods === {{ill|Ōnamuji|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} (later known as [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]]) was pursued by his elder brothers, the {{ill|Eighty Gods|ja|八十神|1=Yasogami|lt=Yasogami (80 gods)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eighty Gods|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasogami|qq=}}, who sought to marry {{ill|Yagami-hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yagami-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Yagami-hime|qq=}} of {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, when Yagami-hime declared her intent to wed Ōnamuji instead, the Eighty Gods became enraged and plotted to kill him. They led Ōnamuji to the foothills of a mountain on the border of {{ill|Hōki Province|ja|伯岐国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, telling him, "A red boar dwells in these mountains. We will drive it down, and you must capture it." As Ōnamuji waited, they instead rolled a burning boulder down the mountain, which he attempted to catch, only to be consumed by the flames and perish.<ref>{{ill|Tamio Tobe|ja|戸部民夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamio Tobe|qq=|1=Tamio Tobe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q38277399}}, ''Japanese Mythology'', pp. 76–77.</ref>
Ōnamuji’s mother, {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, mourned his death and ascended to [[:en:Takamagahara|Takama-ga-hara]], pleading with {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ}} to save him. Kamimusubi sent {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|qq=|1=Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11296639}}, known respectively as Kisagai-hime ('''𧏛'''貝比売)<ref>'''𧏛''' is composed of "討" beneath "虫".</ref> and Umugi-hime (蛤貝比売), who resurrected Ōnamuji through their healing arts.<ref>Tobe, pp. 77–79.</ref>
Upon learning of his survival, the Eighty Gods attempted to kill him again. They cut down a massive tree, split it with wedges, and forced Ōnamuji inside before removing the wedges, crushing him to death. His grieving mother found the tree, split it open, and once again revived him. Fearing for his life, she urged him to flee to {{ill|Oyabiko|ja|大屋毘古神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyabiko|qq=}} of {{ill|Kii Province|ja|木国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 紀伊国|1=Kii Province|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q864053}}.<ref>Tobe, pp. 79–80.</ref>
When Ōnamuji arrived, the Eighty Gods pursued him, demanding his capture. Oyabiko, however, helped him escape by guiding him through a crevice in the trees and directing him to flee to [[:en:Ne-no-kuni|Ne-no-Kuni]], the land of the dead, where {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} resided.<ref>Tobe, p. 81.</ref>
=== Journey to Ne-no-Kuni === At Susanoo’s house in Ne-no-Kuni, Ōnamuji encountered Susanoo’s daughter, {{ill|Suseri-Hime|lt=Suseri-Hime|lt=Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suseribime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スセリビメ}}, and they fell in love at first sight. When she informed her father of the guest, Susanoo scoffed, "He is but an ugly man. He should be called {{ill|Ashihara-shikowo|ja|葦原色許男神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara-shikowo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} instead," and confined him in a chamber filled with serpents. Suseribime, however, secretly gifted him a sacred scarf (比礼, ''hihire'') and instructed him to wave it three times if the snakes attacked. By following her advice, he survived the night.<ref>Tobe, pp. 82–84.</ref>
The next night, Susanoo placed him in a chamber filled with centipedes and bees. Again, Suseribime provided him with a protective scarf, ensuring his safety.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
On the following day, Susanoo challenged Ōnamuji to retrieve an arrow he had shot into a vast field. However, once Ōnamuji entered, Susanoo set fire to the field, attempting to burn him alive. Desperate, Ōnamuji was approached by a mouse, which whispered, "The inside is hollow, the outside is narrow." Understanding the riddle, Ōnamuji stomped on the ground and fell into a hidden burrow, avoiding the flames. The mouse then returned the lost arrow to him. Meanwhile, believing him dead, Suseribime began preparations for his funeral. However, when Susanoo checked the field, he found Ōnamuji alive and holding the arrow.<ref>Tobe, pp. 84–87.</ref>
Satisfied, Susanoo allowed Ōnamuji to enter his house and ordered him to remove lice from his hair. To his horror, the lice were actually centipedes. Cleverly, Ōnamuji chewed up the mukuro nuts (椋の実) given to him by Suseribime and mixed them with red clay, pretending to spit them out. Believing his guest was crushing centipedes in his mouth, Susanoo grew fond of him and eventually dozed off.<ref>Tobe, p. 87.</ref>
Seizing the opportunity, Ōnamuji tied Susanoo’s hair to the pillars of the house, blocked the entrance with a boulder, and fled. He took Susanoo’s {{ill|Ikutachi|ja|生大刀|Ikutachi=Ikutachi|lt=Living Sword|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikutachi|qq=}}, {{ill|Living Bow and Arrows|ja|生弓矢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Living Bow and Arrows|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Suseribime’s {{ill|Heavenly Decree Harp|ja|天詔琴|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Decree Harp|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, carrying Suseribime on his back. However, as they fled, the harp struck a tree, producing a loud sound that woke Susanoo. In his struggle to chase them, he pulled down the pillars with his hair still bound, allowing Ōnamuji to escape.<ref>Tobe, p. 88.</ref>
Susanoo pursued him as far as [[:en:Yomotsu Hirasaka|Yomotsu Hirasaka]], the gateway between the underworld and the living world. There, he stopped and called out to the fleeing Ōnamuji, commanding, "Use the sword and bow I have granted you to drive away the Eighty Gods who defy you. You shall henceforth be named Ōkuninushi, also called {{ill|Utsushikunitama|ja|宇都志国玉神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushikunitama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}, and together with Suseribime, you must build a grand palace and rule over the land." With these words, he bade him farewell.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Ōkuninushi returned to {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where he used his newfound divine weapons to subdue the Eighty Gods, chasing them into the mountains and the riverbanks. With Suseribime as his wife, he built a great palace with pillars rooted in rock and rafters reaching the heavens, thus beginning his rule and shaping the land.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Upon hearing of his rise, Yagami-hime, fearing the wrath of Suseribime, abandoned the child she had borne with Ōnamuji, leaving it in the fork of a tree before returning to her home.<ref>Tobe, p. 92.</ref>
=== The Marriage Quest of Ōkuninushi === {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}, another name for Ōkuninushi, sought to wed [[:en:Nunakawahime|Nunakawahime]] of {{ill|Koshi Province|ja|高志国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koshi Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 越国|1=Koshi Province|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1351611}}. He journeyed to her land, where they exchanged love poems. However, his actions enraged Suseribime, who became fiercely jealous. As he fled from Izumo to [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], he sang a farewell song to Suseribime, who, in turn, responded with a song offering him a farewell cup. The two exchanged cups, signifying their continued connection, and it is said that they remain enshrined to this day.<ref>Tobe, pp. 93–99.</ref> == Analysis == * These tales are found in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]] but not in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. * The myths demonstrate parallels to initiation rites and worldwide mythological motifs, such as the hero's trials and resurrection. * The healing process involving {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|qq=|1=Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11296639}} resembles the use of [[:en:Anadara_broughtonii|ark shell]] powder mixed with [[:en:Meretrix_lusoria|clam]] juice for burn treatment, which aligns with traditional Japanese medicine.<ref>富士川游, 中外医事新報, 1915.</ref> * Nunakawahime's name reflects a connection to [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo Province]]’s [[Kubiki District]], where the {{ill|Nunakawa Shrine|ja|奴奈川神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunakawa Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is dedicated to her.
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Akaiwa Shrine|ja|赤猪岩神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaiwa Shrine|qq=}} (Tottori Prefecture) – Associated with the rock used to kill Ōnamuji.

Believing his guest was crushing centipedes in his mouth, Susanoo grew fond of him and eventually dozed off.<ref>Tobe, p. 87.</ref>
Seizing the opportunity, Ōnamuji tied Susanoo’s hair to the pillars of the house, blocked the entrance with a boulder, and fled. He took Susanoo’s {{ill|Ikutachi|ja|生大刀|Ikutachi=Ikutachi|lt=Living Sword|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikutachi|qq=}}, {{ill|Living Bow and Arrows|ja|生弓矢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Living Bow and Arrows|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Suseribime’s {{ill|Heavenly Decree Harp|ja|天詔琴|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heavenly Decree Harp|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, carrying Suseribime on his back. However, as they fled, the harp struck a tree, producing a loud sound that woke Susanoo. In his struggle to chase them, he pulled down the pillars with his hair still bound, allowing Ōnamuji to escape.<ref>Tobe, p. 88.</ref>
Susanoo pursued him as far as [[:en:Yomotsu Hirasaka|Yomotsu Hirasaka]], the gateway between the underworld and the living world. There, he stopped and called out to the fleeing Ōnamuji, commanding, "Use the sword and bow I have granted you to drive away the Eighty Gods who defy you. You shall henceforth be named Ōkuninushi, also called {{ill|Utsushikunitama|ja|宇都志国玉神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushikunitama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}, and together with Suseribime, you must build a grand palace and rule over the land." With these words, he bade him farewell.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Ōkuninushi returned to {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where he used his newfound divine weapons to subdue the Eighty Gods, chasing them into the mountains and the riverbanks. With Suseribime as his wife, he built a great palace with pillars rooted in rock and rafters reaching the heavens, thus beginning his rule and shaping the land.<ref>Tobe, pp. 90–92.</ref>
Upon hearing of his rise, Yagami-hime, fearing the wrath of Suseribime, abandoned the child she had borne with Ōnamuji, leaving it in the fork of a tree before returning to her home.<ref>Tobe, p. 92.</ref>
=== The Marriage Quest of Ōkuninushi === {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}, another name for Ōkuninushi, sought to wed [[:en:Nunakawahime|Nunakawahime]] of {{ill|Koshi Province|ja|高志国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koshi Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 越国|1=Koshi Province|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1351611}}. He journeyed to her land, where they exchanged love poems. However, his actions enraged Suseribime, who became fiercely jealous. As he fled from Izumo to [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], he sang a farewell song to Suseribime, who, in turn, responded with a song offering him a farewell cup. The two exchanged cups, signifying their continued connection, and it is said that they remain enshrined to this day.<ref>Tobe, pp. 93–99.</ref> == Analysis == * These tales are found in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]] but not in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. * The myths demonstrate parallels to initiation rites and worldwide mythological motifs, such as the hero's trials and resurrection. * The healing process involving {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|qq=|1=Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11296639}} resembles the use of [[:en:Anadara_broughtonii|ark shell]] powder mixed with [[:en:Meretrix_lusoria|clam]] juice for burn treatment, which aligns with traditional Japanese medicine.<ref>富士川游, 中外医事新報, 1915.</ref> * Nunakawahime's name reflects a connection to [[:en:Echigo Province|Echigo Province]]’s [[Kubiki District]], where the {{ill|Nunakawa Shrine|ja|奴奈川神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunakawa Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is dedicated to her.
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Akaiwa Shrine|ja|赤猪岩神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaiwa Shrine|qq=}} (Tottori Prefecture) – Associated with the rock used to kill Ōnamuji.
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=民夫 戸部|title=日本神話: 神々の壮麗なるドラマ|publisher=[[新紀元社]]|year=2003|isbn=9784775302033}}
